titleEn: Cease Your Blame

verses.sadr: دَعْ عَنْكَ لَوْمِي فَإِنَّ اللَّوْمَ إِغْرَاءُ
مِنْ كَفِّ ذَاتِ حِرٍ فِي زِيِّ ذِي ذَكَرٍ

verses.ajuz: فَلَاحَ مِنْ وَجْهِهَا فِي البَيْتِ لَأْلَاءُ

translationEn: Cease your blame of me, for blame only incites me more — and cure me with the very thing that was the disease. A golden wine upon whose courtyard sorrows never descend; if a stone touched it, joy would touch that stone. Poured from the hand of one who rose with her pitcher while the night was dark, and from her face a radiance shone throughout the house.
context: The most famous opening line in Arabic wine poetry (khamriyyat). Abu Nuwas provocatively declares that blame only encourages him further, and that wine itself is both the disease and the cure. This poem became the defining manifesto of the Abbasid bacchic tradition.

titleEn: Pour Me Wine

verses.sadr: أَلَا فَاسْقِنِي خَمْرًا وَقُلْ لِي هِيَ الخَمْرُ
verses.ajuz: وَلَا تَسْقِنِي سِرًّا إِذَا أَمْكَنَ الجَهْرُ

verses.sadr: فَعِيشُكَ بَيْنَ اثْنَيْنِ لَا بُدَّ مِنْهُمَا
فَمَا طَابَتِ الدُّنْيَا وَلَا عِيشُ أَهْلِهَا
وَمَا العَيْشُ إِلَّا سَكْرَةٌ بَعْدَ سَكْرَةٍ
verses.ajuz: فَمَا ضَرَّ أَنْ يَفْنَى إِذَا بَقِيَ السُّكْرُ

translationEn: Pour me wine and tell me plainly it is wine — do not pour it in secret when openness is possible. Your life lies between two certainties: the certainty of death one day, and life that keeps you busy meanwhile. The world and the life of its people are not what is sweet — rather what is sweet in it is wine and intoxication. Life is nothing but one rapture after another, so what harm if it perishes so long as the intoxication remains?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pour me, give me to drink

context: A quintessential Abu Nuwas wine poem that combines hedonism with philosophical reflection on mortality. The demand for openness about drinking was a deliberate challenge to the hypocrisy Abu Nuwas perceived in Abbasid court society.

titleEn: O Moon I Beheld

verses.sadr: يَا قَمَرًا أَبْصَرْتُهُ يَمْشِي بِأَحْسَنِ مَا اكْتَسَى
verses.ajuz: أَعْجَبَنِي حُسْنُ الغُلَامِ فَقُلْتُ لِلسَّاقِي اجْلِسَا

verses.sadr: وَقُلْ لَهُ يَا ذَا الَّذِي أَضْحَى بِوَجْهِكَ مُؤْنِسَا
verses.ajuz: أَدِرْ عَلَيْنَا كَأْسَنَا مَا إِنْ نُرِيدُ لَهَا حَسَا

verses.sadr: فَقَامَ يَسْعَى بَيْنَنَا بِمُدَامَةٍ صِرْفًا حَسَا
verses.ajuz: كَالمِسْكِ فِي نَفَحَاتِهِ وَالتِّبْرِ لَوْنًا أَنْفَسَا

verses.sadr: فِي قَهْوَةٍ مَا مَزَجَتْ إِلَّا بِدَمْعِ مُوَسْوِسَا
verses.ajuz: تَجْلُو الهُمُومَ إِذَا طَغَتْ وَتُزِيلُ مَا كَانَ اخْتَلَسَا

translationEn: O moon I beheld walking in the finest garments — the beauty of the lad delighted me, so I told the cupbearer to sit. I said to him: O you whose face gives us comfort, pass our cup around, for we need no sipping. He rose and moved among us with pure unmixed wine, fragrant as musk in its wafts, more precious than gold in color. A wine that was only ever mixed with a whisperer's tears, that clears away sorrows when they overflow and removes whatever was snatched away. So drink over the rose of cheeks a wine that heals all grief.
context: A classic Abu Nuwas poem combining wine description with the beauty of the cupbearer. The comparison of wine to musk and gold is typical of his ornate style, which influenced the development of badi' (ornamental) rhetoric in Arabic poetry.

titleEn: Bid Farewell to the Ruins

verses.sadr: عَاجٌ بِنَا يَا صَاحِ عَنْ دِمَنِ الهَوَى
verses.ajuz: وَاشْرَبْ عَلَى الوَرْدِ مِنْ حُمَيَّا الصَّهْبَاءِ

verses.sadr: لَا تَبْكِ مِنْ دِمَنٍ وَلَا تَقِفْ بِهَا
وَخُذِ الكُؤُوسَ مِنَ السَّقَاةِ وَلَا تَكُنْ

translationEn: Turn away from the traces of love, O companion, and drink from the golden wine over roses. Do not weep at ruins or stand there lingering — enough of standing in remembrance and weeping. Leave the remains to some old-fashioned lover who cries over a trace with a faded mark. Take the cups from the cupbearers and do not be among those who live in misery and toil. True life is in a cup whose bubbles swirl, and whose clarity melts the hearts of all creation. Wine dispels every hidden sorrow and purifies the souls of all ailments.
context: Abu Nuwas's famous literary rebellion against the pre-Islamic qasida tradition. He mocks the convention of weeping at desert ruins (atlal), declaring that wine poetry is the true art form for the cosmopolitan Abbasid age. This poem helped establish khamriyyat as a major independent genre.

titleEn: When I Die, Bury Me

verses.sadr: إِذَا مَا مُتُّ فَادْفِنِّي إِلَى جَنْبِ كَرْمَةِ

verses.ajuz: أَخَافُ إِذَا مَا مُتُّ أَنْ لَا أَذُوقُهَا
مِنَ الخَمْرِ يَوْمًا إِنَّنِي لَا أُطِيقُهَا

verses.sadr: فَلَعَلَّ رَبِّي أَنْ يَرَانِي يَوْمَ بَعْثٍ
فَإِنْ قَالَ لِي يَوْمَ الحِسَابِ لِمَ اشْتَهَيْتَ
verses.ajuz: أَقُولُ لَهُ إِنَّ المَلِيحَةَ قَدْ سَبَتْنِي

verses.sadr: فَلَا تَبْكِ يَا صَاحِي عَلَيَّ وَأَرِقْ لِي
verses.ajuz: مِنَ الرَّاحِ كَأْسًا وَاجْلِسِ اليَوْمَ عِنْدَهَا

translationEn: When I die, bury me beside a grapevine so its roots may water my bones after death. Do not bury me in the desert, for I fear that when I die I shall never taste wine again. Do not make my grave in a land empty of wine even for a day, for I cannot bear that. Let me die between vine and jar, and do not forget a pitcher as their companion. Perhaps my Lord will see me on the Day of Resurrection drunk upon it, and God will forgive its sin. And if He asks me on the Day of Reckoning why I craved it, I shall say: a beautiful maiden beguiled me. She poured me golden wine until it seemed the earth rose up and the sky came near to greet me. So do not weep for me, my friend, but pour me a cup of wine and sit today beside it.
context: One of Abu Nuwas's most audacious poems, imagining himself drunk on Judgment Day and offering a witty defense before God. The poem's dark humor and irreverence shocked conservative audiences but secured his reputation as the greatest wine poet in Arabic literature.

titleEn: A pure wine laughing in its cup
translationEn: A pure, unmixed wine laughing in its cup — a virgin wine they kept safe from the heat of cooking.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they preserved it, they kept it safe

context: A brief epigrammatic verse praising an uncooked, unmixed wine — a recurring theme in Abu Nuwas's khamriyyat where the purity and naturalness of wine are celebrated over adulterated varieties.

titleEn: O one who places the sandgrouse eggs
translationEn: O you who place the sandgrouse eggs under the ostrich for hatching — had you known what lay beneath them, you would not have escaped a painful pecking. O you who plant with your right hand the tree of loyalty in barren salt flats — all people have become corrupt, so look carefully to yourself and choose well whom you befriend.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: eggs of the sandgrouse (a desert bird)
pecking of the ear, painful strike
you befriend, you take as a brother

context: A satirical poem using extended metaphors of misplaced eggs and trees planted in barren soil to mock someone who cultivates loyalty where none can grow, ending with a pessimistic view of humanity.

titleEn: O you who broke your promise

verses.sadr: ومن أفرط في الهجران ، والإعراض والصد
verses.ajuz: ويا قارون في الكبر ، ويا عرقوب في الوعد

verses.sadr: ويا أحلى من السكر ، والماذي والقند
verses.ajuz: ويا من قلبه أقسى لنا من حجر صلد

verses.sadr: ويا من كالثريا هو بل أبعد في البعد
verses.ajuz: ومن لو كان في المشرب ساوى المزر بالشهد

verses.sadr: ومن لو كان في الطيب لكان العنبر الهندي
verses.ajuz: ومن لو كان في الريحان ما كان سوى الورد

translationEn: O you who broke your promise and turned away from your covenant, who went to excess in estrangement, indifference, and rejection — O Qarun in arrogance and Arqub in promises. O you whom I dare not name nor reveal your secrets. O you sweeter than musk, softer than butter, sweeter than sugar and honey and candy — yet your heart toward us is harder than solid stone. O you who are like the Pleiades, nay even further in distance. You who, were you among drinks, would make even sour wine equal honey. You who, were you among perfumes, would be Indian ambergris itself. You who, were you among flowers, would be nothing but the rose.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: broke the promise, failed to keep one's word
Korah (Qarun), biblical figure proverbial for arrogance
Arqub, proverbial figure for breaking promises

context: A complex ghazal of longing addressed to a beloved who is simultaneously beautiful beyond compare and cruelly distant, using a series of paradoxical comparisons that mix the sweetest and bitterest of experiences.

titleEn: I was once patient and steadfast

verses.ajuz: سوى ما ترى من نحولي شهودا

translationEn: I was once patient and composed, strong against whatever befell me, firm and resolute. But love has reduced me to a state where I cannot lift even a twig from the ground with my hand. What excuse does one have who is now capable of nothing but seeking the road to her generosity? She continues for me rejection after rejection, stringing together rebuffs one after another. She wants nothing more, from all that I say, than the visible proof of my wasting away.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my wasting away, my thinness from longing

context: A ghazal of unrequited love in which Abu Nuwas contrasts his former strength with the debilitating weakness that love has brought upon him, lamenting a cruel beloved who takes pleasure only in his visible suffering.

titleEn: She of the rosy, rose-red cheek
translationEn: She with the rose-flushed cheek, bewitching in her nakedness — people have contemplated in her beauties that never run dry. Beauty in every part of her recurs and is repeated; some of it is at its end while some is ever being born anew. And whenever you return to it, it is found more admirable upon returning. So drink your wine to the face of a full moon, one satiated but without drunken excess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a rose-red cheek, a blushing cheek
raucously drunk, behaving badly from wine

context: A poem blending ghazal and khamriyyat themes, praising a beautiful face while urging measured drinking — the beloved's face becomes the occasion for wine, reflecting Abu Nuwas's typical fusion of erotic and bacchic poetry.

titleEn: Two lovers whose cheeks intertwined

verses.sadr: نفعل في المسجد ما لم يكن

translationEn: Two lovers whose cheeks intertwined at the kissing of the Black Stone — they satisfied their longing without sinning, as if they had arranged a secret meeting! Had the crowd not pushed them apart, they would not have come to their senses till the very end of the pillar. We each spent the time covering our face, on the side facing the other, with our hand — doing in the mosque what the pious never do in a mosque.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the kissing, the pressing of lips
they satisfied their longing, found relief
they sin, they commit an offense
the pillar, the buttress (of the Kaaba)

context: A provocative poem in which Abu Nuwas describes two lovers exploiting the ritual kissing of the Black Stone during the Hajj to meet secretly — a characteristically irreverent piece that blends the sacred and profane.

titleEn: I shall thank memory for what it does for me

verses.ajuz: وتمثيلها لي من أحب على البعد

verses.sadr: تمثل لي أن لا أقول على النوى
verses.ajuz: فيا ليت شعري ما الذي أحدثت بعدي ؟ !

verses.sadr: لأني ، وإن كانت من الناس ، واثق

translationEn: I shall be grateful to memory for what it does for me — representing to me, across the distance, the one I love. Recollection brings him close, until I almost see him before me in all his states. Indeed, memory almost becomes like direct beholding, were it not for the desolation of his absence. It conjures for me that I need not speak of the separation — and I find myself wondering: what has he done since I left? For though he is like other people, I am confident in myself to remain faithful to our bond.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a good deed, a favor rendered
I behold him, I see him directly

context: A reflective ghazal in which Abu Nuwas meditates on how memory of a beloved substitutes for presence, almost equaling it — but the desolation of loss undermines even memory's consolation.

titleAr: قالوا : تنسك بعد الحج ، قلت لهم :
titleEn: They said become pious after the pilgrimage

verses.sadr: قالوا : تنسك بعد الحج ، قلت لهم :
فإن سلمت ، وما قلبي على ثقة
ما شئت من بلد تدنو منازهه
ما أبعد النسك من قلب نقسمه

verses.ajuz: تقول ذا شرهم ، بل ذاك ، بل هذا
ولا ترى قائلا من ذا ، ولا ماذا . .

translationEn: They said: become devout after the pilgrimage. I replied: I hope in God, but I fear Tayzinabadhah (a wine village). I fear the vine branch will dispute with me for the slack of its rope, even if I hurry away. For even if I escape unscathed — and my heart is not confident of safety — I will not survive Baghdad. What a city it is, with its pleasure gardens nearby, yet filled with clans and tribes of revelers! How far is piety from a heart divided between Qutrubbul, the villages of Bana, and Kulwadhah (all wine regions). A people who counsel each other in virtue among themselves — you say this one is their worst, but no, that one, no, this one! They are not like those whose gathering you pass by while being pushed through with restraint and discretion, where no reproaching ear pries in, and no one asks 'who is this?' or 'what is going on?'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to become pious, to adopt ascetic devotion
Qutrubbul, a village near Baghdad famous for wine

context: One of Abu Nuwas's most famous self-deprecating poems, in which he humorously acknowledges the impossibility of reform after the Hajj, citing his addiction to the wine culture of Baghdad and its surrounding villages as an insurmountable obstacle to piety.

titleEn: The veil of night is now lifted
translationEn: The veil of night is lifted now — so drink, for the first light of dawn has appeared. A pure wine that no hand has pressed and no storms have polluted. It leaps when water appears before it, like the sparks thrown off by a bellows. A noble wine, least of whose ancestors are Khosrow and Shapur, if one traces its lineage. Time folded its days over it, and fate overlooked it. It kept refining itself until, when the jar was half empty, it came forth like a spirit whose essence no substance could contain and no light could measure. It is poured for you by a witty, roguish youth — practiced at serving, a skilled expert.
context: A dawn drinking poem (subhiyyah) urging a companion to drink at daybreak, praising the noble lineage of the wine as if it were an aristocrat of royal descent, and the skilled cup-bearer who serves it.

titleEn: Rejection made me taste my own mismanagement
translationEn: Rejection made me taste the bitterness of my own poor management, for my aim is beyond my means. That is because I am a young man besotted with what settles pure in clear glass vessels — a Khindris wine whose bridle is clay and whose garment is pitch. It shines in the cup from its brilliance, decorated with firm, intricate images, as if a phantasmal player, when darkness falls, toys with the finest lute. And a kohl-eyed, dark-pupiled youth among noble, expert young men, in a gathering overlooking trees whose apples smile toward the good, with a bird perched on a branch gladdened by the chirping of sparrows. We spent our day and night plucking on the rooftop with lutes, until we saw the darkness retreating and the sun wheeling through its chambers. And when the time of our forenoon prayer had come around, we rose to pray — without even saying the opening takbir.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a type of aged wine (from Greek)
having dark, brilliant eyes (a beauty marker)

context: A long descriptive poem combining self-mockery about unrequited love with a vivid scene of wine-drinking and music on a rooftop lasting through the night, ending irreverently with a prayer said without the proper opening formula.

titleEn: Seize your youth before grey hair and disgrace

verses.sadr: من قهوة لم تزل تخفى ، ويحجبها
من قعر أجوف ، ذي ساق بلا قدم ،

verses.ajuz: من مسك دارين فيها نفحة الغار

verses.sadr: يا رب ليل طرقنا بيت صاحبها

verses.ajuz: يسعى إلى شبح في كن أستار
في الكأس تحت الدجى من زندها الواري
من بين ذي قرطق ، أو ذات زنار
إن ضل في ظلمة عن قصده الساري

verses.sadr: كأنها عند مس الماء من جزع
في حلبة الحان جان خلفه شهب
والكأس تمسكها من أن تراع ؛ فما
تبدو لنا عطلا ، حتى إذا مزجت
verses.ajuz: حلى لها المزج سمطي در قسطار
في غير سلك ، ولم يوثق بمسمار

verses.sadr: من بين بم إلى مثنى ومثلثه
verses.ajuz: وما خلا ذاك من أصوات أوتار

verses.sadr: نيطت إلى بدن كالخلق ليس له
يحكي صداه مجيد الصوت إذ نطقت

translationEn: Seize your youth before grey hair and disgrace arrive, and pass the cup from one vintage to another. Of a wine that has long been hidden, veiled like noble women through age after age, kept secluded in the shelter of the wine-seller's house. It came from the depths of a hollow vessel, with a stem but no foot, attached to a great-bellied, thundering body whose interior is pitch-lined, with pottery built atop it. Within it rests a vintage red as a rooster's eye, clear, with a breath of Darin's musk and a waft of laurel. How many a night we came to the keeper's dwelling — a band of men like stars in the sky, all free-born. He rose to search for the wine in the dark, making his way toward a shape behind the veil of curtains. Some among them, seeing the wonder glowing in the cup beneath the darkness from its ember-like gleam, said: the daytime sun! But how can this be the hour of its rising? Others said: light from a fire! Until at last maids passed the cups — some veiled in a robe, some girt with a sash — bringing a glowing vessel to guide the nobles in darkness. Like something trembling at the touch of water, while the water itself recoils from it in flight, in the ring of the tavern a runner followed by sparks, startled by a passing figure. The cup holds itself in check from being startled, constantly going back and forth. A bride from a ruby-hued chamber sheltering beneath its canopy full moons of light. It appears to us plain and unadorned, but when mixed, the mixing adorns it with two strands of Qasdar pearls — like a necklace arranged in a collar without a thread, fastened without a nail. And a friend among the maidens of the quarter, gladdened by the varied sounds of plucked strings — from the bass to the double and triple strings, and all other string sounds — attached to a body like a living form but without soul, fashioned from timber. The craftsman found it in a thicket, chose its finest part, and kept sawing at it. With its curved scroll and its long neck, its craft is as if magic, yet no sorcerer's knot has touched it. Its pegs are so complete in form they seem like fingers moved by a working joint. Its resonance mimics the finest voice when its tones speak in the language of drum and flute. Such was our custom before grey hair descended upon us — but we hope still for the forgiveness of the Most Forgiving.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Darin, a port famous for musk
ruby (used to describe the wine's color)
the Most Forgiving (an epithet of God)

context: One of Abu Nuwas's longest and most elaborate khamriyyat, containing an extended description of a wine-drinking gathering with cup-bearers, tavern keepers, and a detailed digression on the craft of the lute, ending with a characteristic note of repentance.

titleAr: أبحت حريم الكأس إذ كنت مثريا ،
titleEn: I opened the sanctum of the cup when I was wealthy

verses.sadr: أبحت حريم الكأس إذ كنت مثريا ،
وثقت بعفو الله عن كل مسلم ،
verses.ajuz: فلست عن الصهباء ما عشت مقصرا
له شفة من مصها مص سكرا

verses.sadr: فلو أنه يقظان ، أو في منامه
يخر لصرف الكأس في السكر ساجدا ،
فقلت له : هب لي من النوم رقدة ،

translationEn: I opened the sanctuary of the cup when I was prosperous, and gave it up after I became poor. Had my wealth been equal to my pleasures, I would have made the pleasure-seekers forget Khosrow and Caesar. I trust in God's pardon for every Muslim — so I shall not be remiss in drinking the amber wine as long as I live. And a dark-eyed youth, headstrong and unbridled, you would take him for a green basil branch swaying in the breeze. With heavy-lidded eyes and a girdle around his waist, his lip is one that, if kissed, tastes of sugar. Were he awake or dreaming and granting his loyalty to a blind man, the blind man would see. He falls prostrate before the pure cup in his drunkenness, and if it is mixed, he prays over it and proclaims God's greatness. He passed the cup among us in greeting, clothing it in a robe of red wine. I said to him as the cup gleamed in his hand and the flagon bled into it and gurgled: by God, did you serve me wine or soaked raisins? He replied with distaste: saffron-colored water. I said to him: grant me a nap of sleep, and we shall drink it again at dawn.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unbridled, headstrong (lit. with the bridle removed)
the flagon bled (wine poured out like blood from a nose)

context: A khamriyyah describing a wine session with a beautiful cup-bearer, mixing Abu Nuwas's characteristic wit and irreverence, including his theological self-justification for drinking and a playful exchange with the wine-server.

titleEn: Abandoning the morning drink is a sign of decline
translationEn: Abandoning the morning drink is a mark of decline — so make the wine-seller's abode your permanent residence. Let not the glowing sun rise with its light but that you are already the scandal of the house.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a settled place, a permanent abode

context: A brief, epigrammatic poem urging the morning drink (subuh) as a badge of honor — a characteristic Abu Nuwas inversion in which the tavern becomes one's true home and sobriety is presented as the real disgrace.

titleAr: إني صرفت الهوى إلى قمر إني صرفت الهوى إلى قمر
titleEn: I have turned my passion toward a moon

verses.sadr: إني صرفت الهوى إلى قمر إني صرفت الهوى إلى قمر
إذا تأملته تعاظمك الإقرار في أنه من البشر
verses.ajuz: ثم يعود الإنكار معرفة منك إذا قسته إلى الصور

translationEn: I have turned my passion toward a moon — one that does not challenge the eyes with his gaze. When you contemplate him, the acknowledgment overwhelms you that he is of human kind, then the denial returns as a kind of recognition when you compare him to other forms of beauty. The ground of hearts lies open to him; he takes from it the finest of its fruit.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I directed my passion, I turned my love toward
a moon (metaphor for a beautiful person)
overwhelms you, seems too great for you
the finest of the fruit (metaphor for the best of hearts)

context: A ghazal in praise of a beloved of moon-like beauty, whose perfection is so overwhelming that the poet can barely believe he is human — a classic trope in the Abbasid ghazal tradition elevated here with philosophical paradox.

titleAr: يا ذا الذي عن جنان ظل يخبرني ،
titleEn: O you who has been telling me about Jinan

verses.sadr: يا ذا الذي عن جنان ظل يخبرني ،
verses.ajuz: بالله قل وأعد يا طيب الخبر

verses.sadr: قال : اشكتك ، قالت : ما بليت به
verses.ajuz: أراه من حيثما أقبلت في أثري

verses.sadr: ويعمل الطرف نحوي إن مررت به

verses.ajuz: في الموضع الخلو ينطق من الحصر

verses.sadr: ما زال يفعل في هذا ويدمنه
verses.ajuz: حتى لقد صار من همي ومن وطري

translationEn: O you who has been telling me about Jinan — by God, say it again, O bearer of sweet news! He said: she complained of you. She said: the affliction I have suffered is that I see him everywhere I turn, following me. He fixes his gaze upon me whenever I pass, until his intensity of looking makes me blush. And if I stop so that he may speak to me in some private place, he falls speechless from shyness. He has persisted in this, continuing without relent, until he has truly become one of my concerns and my desires.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the affliction I have suffered with him
he works his glance, he directs his gaze

context: A ghazal in which the poet asks a messenger to relay what a woman named Jinan said about his persistent pursuit, and she describes him as a shy but obsessive admirer — a charming reversal in which the beloved herself narrates his love.

titleEn: A full-breasted maid of the palace servants

verses.sadr: كلفت بما أبصرت من حسن وجهها
verses.ajuz: زمانا ، وما حب الكواعب من أمري

verses.sadr: فما زلت بالأشعار في كل مشهد

verses.ajuz: على غير ميعاد ، إلي مع العصر
إلى الله من وصل الرجال مع الخمر

verses.sadr: فقلت : اشربي إن كان هذا محرما ،
verses.ajuz: ففي عنقي يا ريم وزرك مع وزري

verses.sadr: فما زلت في رفق ، ونفسي تقول لي :

translationEn: A full-breasted maiden from the palace servants captivated me with the beauty of her neck, face, and throat. Dressed in a page's style, of Barmakid descent, with decorated temples and a cropped head of hair. I became infatuated for a time with the beauty of her face — though love of buxom girls is not my usual inclination. I kept softening her with poems at every occasion, for poetry is one of the bonds of magic. Until she agreed to union and came to me unexpectedly at the afternoon hour. I welcomed her and our cups went round with a wine the color of turmeric or glowing embers. She said: is that wine, perhaps? I am innocent before God of keeping company with men over wine! I said: drink — and if this is forbidden, then its sin falls on my neck together with my own, O gazelle! I sought something from her, and she said with tears: I would die from it then — her tears flowing. I kept gently at it while my soul whispered to me: a young virgin girl! And that is the anxiety of a virgin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: of the Barmakid family (a noble Persian clan)
buxom young women (lit. those with developed busts)
a wine cooled by the north wind, a fine wine

context: A narrative ghazal recounting Abu Nuwas's encounter with a palace servant girl of Barmakid lineage, in which wine, poetry, and seduction intertwine, culminating in a tender note of hesitation about deflowering a virgin.

titleAr: إلى الله أشكو حب من جل نيله
titleEn: To God I complain of love for one hard to attain

verses.sadr: إلى الله أشكو حب من جل نيله
صبرت لها حتى إذا ما تفجرت

translationEn: To God I complain of love for one who is difficult to attain — only speech from behind a wall reaches me from her. I was patient until the springs of passion burst open around me and my wine-induced daze flew away. I took my sword as companion and then came to her at night — crossing terrifying obstacles, with every restraint cast off. When we met, I saw her hands were short, though once they had not been short. If one eye was stingy about kissing its sister, at least a hand was not stingy about loosening a sash. We almost went further... but only our lips exchanged a mixture of sugar and wine. I bade her farewell at dawn, not forgetting her coyness — and she had exchanged her ring for my bracelet.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: too great to attain, difficult of access
the springs of passion burst open
one who has cast off restraint (lit. removed the bridle)

context: A daring ghazal narrating a nocturnal visit to a beloved guarded behind walls, blending bravado and tenderness, and ending with the token exchange of jewelry — a sign of mutual attachment despite the incomplete union.

titleAr: قنعت ، إذ نلت من أحبابي النظرا
titleEn: I was content when I gained but a glance from my beloved

verses.sadr: قنعت ، إذ نلت من أحبابي النظرا
verses.ajuz: وقلت : يا رب ما أعطيت ذا بشرا

verses.sadr: لم يبق مني ، من قرني إلى قدمي
verses.ajuz: شيء سوى القلب إلا هنأ البصرا

verses.sadr: يا ويح من لا يبالي عين مبصره ،
verses.ajuz: ألا ترى معه شمسا ولا قمرا

translationEn: I was content when I gained a mere glance from my beloved, and said: O Lord, You have granted no human being what You gave me. From my crown to my sole, nothing remained in me — save only my heart — that was not gladdened by what my eyes beheld. Woe to one who does not care for the vision of his beholding — one for whom neither sun nor moon is worth seeing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was content, I was satisfied
the glance, the look, the gaze
cares for, heeds, pays attention to

context: A brief ghazal reflecting the mystical intensity of visual rapture — the poet is content with a single glance, considering it a divine gift, and pities those who cannot appreciate beauty in the world around them.

titleEn: Say to Ibrahim a taunting word

verses.sadr: إن قلت : ما تترك ؟ قال : برا ،
verses.ajuz: أو قلت : ما ترهب ؟ قال : بحرا

verses.sadr: أو قلت : ما تقول ؟ قال : شرا

translationEn: Say to Ibrahim a taunting, scornful word: you have outstripped me in heresy and disbelief! If you ask him: what do you abandon? He says: dry land. If you ask: what do you fear? He says: the sea. If you ask: what do you say? He says: evil. May my Lord burn him in flame and embers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may He make him taste (the fire)

context: A brief satirical poem targeting a man named Ibrahim, mocking him with rapid-fire rhetorical questions whose answers are all negative, ending with a curse — a typical piece of Abu Nuwas's sharp-tongued hija.

titleAr: من يزدري الكبش في الدنيا ويحقره
titleEn: Whoever scorns the ram in this world

verses.sadr: من يزدري الكبش في الدنيا ويحقره
verses.ajuz: فإنه رأس أهل النار في النار

translationEn: Whoever scorns and belittles the ram in this world — for it is the head of the people of Hellfire in the fire. A man may be too weak to earn his companion's anger, yet a ram can bring down upon itself the wrath of the Creator, the Maker.
context: A moralizing epigram with religious undertones, likely referring to the ram that is the leader of the inhabitants of Hell in Islamic tradition, and contrasting human weakness with the capacity of even an animal to incur divine wrath.

titleEn: A young man who adorns his loaf with earrings
translationEn: A young man who adorns his loaf of bread with earrings and pendant, and two anklets of beads and pearls. When he loses his loaf, he weeps over it as al-Khansa wept when she was bereaved of Sakhr. And for the sake of his loaf he would have front teeth knocked out, and a war like the Battle of Badr.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an ear-pendant, a type of earring
al-Khansa, a famous pre-Islamic elegiac poetess
Sakhr, al-Khansa's brother, killed in battle

context: A satirical poem mocking a miser who treats his bread loaf with the devotion normally reserved for a beloved or a fallen hero, using hyperbolic comparisons to the great elegist al-Khansa and the Battle of Badr for comic effect.

titleAr: رأيت قدور الناس سودا من الصلى ،
titleEn: I saw people's cooking pots blackened by fire

verses.sadr: رأيت قدور الناس سودا من الصلى ،

verses.ajuz: سليم ، صحيح ، لم يصبه أذى الجمر
ثلاثا كنقط الثاء من نقط الحبر
لأخرجت ما فيها على طرف الظفر

verses.sadr: إذا ما تنادوا للرحيل سعى بها ،

translationEn: I saw the cooking pots of people blackened from the fire, while the cooking pot of the Raqashiyyun shines white as a full moon. Its ladle shows that its stave is sound and whole, untouched by the harm of burning coals. They keep it overnight for the supplicants at their courtyard — three times, like the three dots of the letter tha in ink. Were it filled to the brim with fresh, generous meat, they would empty it to the very tip of a fingernail. It goes out to the tribe of al-Rabb and Darim and Amr, and the men of the Namir come to share from it. The tribe of Qays has a share of its ladle-portions, and Qahtan, and the noble tall men of the Banu Bakr. When they call out for departure, a yearling calf from the offspring of al-Dhar runs ahead of them with it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the fire, heat of the flame
those who come seeking hospitality and food

context: A poem of praise for the tribe of the Raqashiyyun, celebrating their legendary generosity through the central image of their ever-glowing, unstained cooking pot — a classic emblem of Arab tribal hospitality in panegyric poetry.

titleEn: Return them, O Muhammad ibn Zuhayr
translationEn: Return them, O Muhammad ibn Zuhayr — O torment of thieves and rogues! Ordinary thieves steal at night, but this one steals from people openly in broad daylight. My poems have become fiefs parceled out to nobles — why? What for? For the scarcity of poems?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a fief, a land grant, an allotment
nobles, the best of people, distinguished men

context: A brief satirical poem accusing a man named Muhammad ibn Zuhayr of plagiarizing or misappropriating Abu Nuwas's poetry and distributing it to nobles — a complaint about poetic theft delivered with characteristic wit.

titleEn: I see myself living among the living while most of me is dead

verses.ajuz: على الدهر ميت قد تخرمه الدهر

verses.sadr: فما لم يمت مني بما مات ناهض ،
verses.ajuz: فبعضي لبعضي دون قبر البلى قبر

verses.sadr: فيا رب قد أحسنت عودا وبدأة
فمن كان ذا عذر لديك ، وحجة ،
verses.ajuz: فعذري إقراري بأن ليس لي عذر

translationEn: I see myself living among the living while the greater part of me lies dead — the one whom time has claimed. What in me has not died by what rises and dies again — then part of me is a grave to another part, even before the grave of decay. O my Lord, You have been good to me in both return and beginning, yet my gratitude has not risen to meet Your goodness. Whoever has an excuse before You, a proof — then my excuse is my admission that I have no excuse.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: time has claimed him, time has cut him down
what rises, what stirs and strives
in return and in beginning, repeatedly and from the start

context: A profound zuhd poem in which Abu Nuwas reflects on death and the friends he has lost, feeling himself already partially dead, and ends with the famous paradox of his excuse being the confession that he has no excuse — a classic of Abbasid penitential poetry.

titleAr: طوى الموت ما بيني وبين محمد ،
titleEn: Death has folded away what lay between me and Muhammad

verses.sadr: طوى الموت ما بيني وبين محمد ،

verses.ajuz: أحاديث نفس ، ما لها ، الدهر ، ذاكر
فلم يبق لي شيء عليه أحاذر

verses.sadr: لئن عمرت دور بمن لا أوده ،

translationEn: Death has folded away what lay between me and Muhammad — and there is no unfolding what fate has rolled up. There is no connection left but a tear one sustains through the inner conversations of the soul — conversations that time will remember no more. I used to fear for him nothing but death alone; now nothing remains for me to fear on his behalf. Though houses may be filled with those I do not love, the graves have been filled with those I do.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I fear for, I dread for

context: An elegy for a companion named Muhammad, one of Abu Nuwas's most moving short poems, expressing the completeness of loss — the beloved is gone, and even the fear of losing him has been consumed by death's finality.

titleEn: With what shall I satirize you? I do not know!

verses.sadr: إذا فكرت في عرضك أشفقت على شعري

translationEn: With what shall I satirize you? I do not know! My tongue will not flow against you. When I think of your honor, I take pity on my poetry.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I satirize you, I lampoon you
I took pity, I felt compassion for

context: A witty three-line epigram that is actually a devastating insult: the poet claims he cannot satirize his target because to do so would degrade the poetry itself — the ultimate hija that implies the subject is beneath the dignity of verse.

titleEn: I was devoted to riding the nag until
translationEn: I was devoted to riding the nag, until the high price of barley damaged my purse. So I switched to mules — but they escaped my means too, and I moved from mules to donkeys. But even donkeys were beyond me, so I ended up walking, dragging my leg like a man with a broken bone. There is nothing wrong with me — praise be to God — no broken bone, only the loss of the prince's provision of a mount.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a nag, a non-Arab horse (of Central Asian breed)
the prince's provision of a mount, a riding animal given as a gift

context: A humorous poem charting Abu Nuwas's comic descent from horse to mule to donkey to walking, with the punchline revealing that his infirmity is not physical but financial — the loss of the prince's patronage gift of a mount.

titleEn: Be patient with the bitter events of time

verses.sadr: يا ليت شعري ! كيف أنت على
أو ليت شعري ! كيف أنت ، إذا
أو ليت شعري ! كيف أنت ، إذا
أن لا أكون قصدت رشدي أو

verses.ajuz: أسفي على ما فات من عمري !

translationEn: Be patient with the bitter events of time — for you shall praise the outcome of patience. Prepare a resting place for yourself before your death, and lay up provision for the day when provisions are weighed against each other. It is as if your family have called out to you and you could not hear, the death-rattle filling your chest. As if they have perfumed you with the scent worn by those who are perishing. As if they have turned you over onto the bier, into the darkness of the grave. Would that I knew — how are you on the surface of the bier, not knowing? Would that I knew — how are you when you are washed with camphor and lote-tree leaves? Would that I knew — how are you when the reckoning is laid out on the morning of the Resurrection? What argument do I have for what I have done? What do I say to my Lord? What excuse is mine — that I did not seek my own guidance, or face what I had turned my back upon in my affairs? What a shame for what I have earned, and what grief for what has passed of my life!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: prepare, smooth the way, make ready
with the death-rattle in the chest
lote-tree leaves (used in washing the dead)
the Resurrection, the Day of Gathering

context: One of Abu Nuwas's most celebrated zuhd poems, a sustained meditation on death, the washing and burial of the body, and the terror of the Day of Judgment, ending with heartfelt regret — a poem that stands in stark contrast to his wine poetry.

titleAr: أيا من ليس لي منه مجير ،
titleEn: O You from Whom I have no refuge

verses.sadr: أيا من ليس لي منه مجير ،

translationEn: O You from Whom I have no refuge — by Your pardon I seek shelter from Your punishment. I am the servant who confesses every sin, and You are the Master, the Lord, the All-Forgiving. If You punish me, it is for the evil of my deeds; and if You forgive, You are fully worthy of that. I flee to You from You — and where else would one who seeks refuge from You flee, but to You?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a protector, one who grants refuge
by Your pardon, through Your forgiveness
I seek refuge, I ask for protection
the one who confesses, the one who acknowledges
the Lord, the Master, the Patron

context: One of the most famous short religious poems in Arabic literature, attributed to Abu Nuwas as a prayer of repentance — the final couplet with its theological paradox of fleeing to God from God has been celebrated by scholars across the centuries.

titleEn: Will you not visit the graves one morning
translationEn: Will you not visit the graves one morning, and hear what the graves tell you? For if their silence were stirred to call out, it would be as if the hidden interiors of their depths had turned inside out.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to call out to one another, to cry aloud
what is hidden, the absent interior
surfaces, outer faces, what is visible

context: A brief but powerful zuhd poem urging the reader to contemplate the graves as a moral reminder — the image of the hidden depths of graves turning outward suggests the revelation of all that is concealed, a call to remember death and its lessons.

titleEn: O Children of Weakness and Folly

verses.sadr: وبني البعد في الطباع على القرب في الصور
verses.ajuz: والشكول التي تباين في الطول والقصر

verses.sadr: أحتساء من الحرام وختما على الصرر
verses.ajuz: أين من كان قبلكم من ذوي البأس والخطر
سبقونا إلى الرحيل ، وإنا على الأثر

verses.sadr: قد نقلتم من القصور إلى ظلمة الحفر
verses.ajuz: حيث لا نضرب القباب عليكم ، ولا الحجر

translationEn: O you children of deficiency and moral lessons, children of weakness and frailty — you who are distant in nature though near in appearance. Where are those who came before you, the mighty and the daring? Ask the cities about them and seek out their news. They departed before us on that final journey, and we follow in their footsteps. Death strikes faster than the blink of an eye. You will be moved to tombs of earth, from palaces to the darkness of the grave. May God have mercy on the Muslim who remembered God and restrained himself, and may God forgive the one who feared and clothed himself in caution.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: lessons, moral warnings drawn from events
bundles of money, purses tied with cord
the trace, the footstep left behind

context: A rare ascetic poem (zuhd) from Abu Nuwas, admonishing contemporaries about the transience of life and the certainty of death. Despite his fame for wine poetry, Abu Nuwas also composed serious devotional and moral verse.

titleEn: Counsel to Oneself on Patience

verses.sadr: أكبر الأشياء عن أصغر عفو الله أصغر
verses.ajuz: ليس للإنسان ، إلا ما قضى الله وقدر

translationEn: The poet addresses himself by name — 'O Nuwas, show gravity and composure, and be patient.' Time has harmed you in some things, but what has pleased you is far greater. O you of great sin, God's forgiveness exceeds even your greatest transgression — for even the smallest of God's pardons surpasses the greatest of all worldly things. There is nothing for man except what God has decreed and ordained, and no creature has true governance of affairs — God alone is the Disposer of all things.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: show gravity and dignity, carry yourself with gravitas
show composure, act with beautiful conduct
time, fate, the course of the ages
the Disposer, the One who manages and arranges all affairs

context: A self-admonishing poem in which Abu Nuwas addresses his own soul by name, urging patience and reliance on God's vast forgiveness. The theological emphasis on divine predestination and the immensity of God's pardon gives the poem a distinctly devotional character.

titleEn: The Seeker of God Shall Triumph

verses.sadr: فارغب إلى الله ، لا إلى بشر
وارغب إلى الله ، لا إلى جسد

translationEn: O seeker of God, you have attained victory and a wholesome, untainted gift. Turn your desire toward God, not toward a mortal who is ever-changing and decaying; not toward a body that passes from youth to old age. He whose petitioners are never disappointed is of a nature unlike that of mortal men — His essence is unlike human essence. Do you busy yourself with trivialities while the guarantee of safety from hellfire rests in your very hands?
context: A devotional poem contrasting the transience of human wealth and body with the permanence of divine generosity. The final verse — challenging the reader directly about hellfire — is a sharp homiletic turn characteristic of Abu Nuwas's zuhd poetry.

titleEn: Going Out with Keen Grey Falcons
translationEn: The poet sets out at dawn with keen, grey-blue falcons — pure-bred, with fine feathers and markings — trained in the open grounds of Nu'man, hunting for provisions and reward. They adorn the falconer's hand and glove through many a long and swift pursuit. One falcon, dubbed Abu Kazzaz, is bold and experienced, abundantly skilled, having been trained in fortified grounds and tuned by the open-field cricket's call. He cleaves through the middle of the skies with curved, accurate talons like the awls of a skillful cobbler, hunting alone without an escort. Indeed, the well-bred falcon trained by trainers of hawks is the finest companion in times of need.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abundant in strikes, prolific at hunting
need, want, a moment of poverty or difficulty

context: A poem in praise of the art of falconry (الصيد بالجوارح), which was a prestige pursuit in Abbasid court culture. Abu Nuwas deploys highly technical hunting vocabulary to describe the falcon's qualities, demonstrating his mastery of the genre beyond wine poetry.

titleEn: The Falcon Surpasses the Sparrowhawk
translationEn: The sparrowhawk is not without merit, but people unanimously prefer the falcon — the falcon hunts the great crane without hesitation, while the sparrowhawk's greatest effort amounts to no more than a chirping chick. This brief epigram uses the two hunting birds as a metaphor for comparing levels of excellence.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: falcon, the prized hunting bird of the Abbasid courts
crane, a large and difficult bird to hunt
does not waver, does not turn back
a chirping or jumping young chick

context: A witty two-verse epigram (نتفة) contrasting two hunting birds to illustrate degrees of excellence. The compressed comparison is characteristic of Abu Nuwas's mastery of concise satirical and observational verse.

titleEn: The Abandoned Tavern of Wine Companions

verses.sadr: مساحب من جر الزقاق على الثرى ،
لم أدر من هم ؟ غير ما شهدت به

translationEn: The poet pauses before an abandoned gathering-place of wine companions who have departed at nightfall, leaving fresh and faded traces — drag marks from wine skins hauled across the soil, and scattered bundles of basil both fresh and dry. He held his own companions there, renewing their bonds, and he is inclined to linger at such places again. He dwelt there four days, wine circling in a golden Persian cup adorned with paintings inherited from Khosrow, its sides depicting white antelopes chased by mounted archers. The wine fills whatever opens in their collars, and water fills what turns in their caps.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine companions, drinking friends (plural of نديم)
they departed at night, traveled through the darkness
drag marks, traces left by dragging
white antelopes, gazelles (a classical beauty metaphor)

context: A khamriyyah that opens with the classical elegiac motif of the abandoned campsite (أطلال), here transformed into a deserted wine companions' tavern. The Persian cup bearing Khosrow's painted imagery reflects the cosmopolitan Abbasid cultural setting.

titleEn: Time Gnawed Me and Love Struck Me Down

verses.sadr: أضلني بعدما كنت في العبادة قسا
verses.ajuz: لا أستفيق صلاة ، ولا أفتر درسا

verses.sadr: هلا طرقت ، ولم تلق في القراطق شمسا
verses.ajuz: فقلت : يا نور عيني ، خلست عقلي خلسا

verses.sadr: فما تمالك حتى افترى علي وخسا
verses.ajuz: فاسود وجهي منه ، حتى تحول نقسا

translationEn: Time has consumed the poet bit by bit, and love has struck him down further into weakness. His beloved's love has attached itself to his heart like a saddle-cloth to a beast, mixing with his soul until it became his very breath. Love led him astray after he had been devout — never missing a prayer, never ceasing to study. His reason flew away, leaving no trace; he blames his own glancing eye. He pleads to his beloved: 'Light of my eyes, you have stolen my mind — return my life with a bite of your mouth and a lick.' The beloved rejects him, lying about him and cursing; the poet's face darkens with shame as dark as ink. Time, he laments, brings him nothing but insults and diminishment, and no beloved he has known is harder-hearted or crueler.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: nibbling, gnawing bit by bit (as a predator gnaws)
relapse, collapse, being struck down again
a saddlecloth; metaphor for something always attached
a bishop or Christian monk; used for an intensely devout person
garments, tunics (collar area near the throat)
ink, pitch-black (turning as dark as ink with shame)
diminishment, undervaluing, treating someone as worthless

context: An intense ghazal in which Abu Nuwas charts the progression from devout ascetic life to complete mental dissolution through love. The ironic self-portrait as a fallen devout scholar (قس) is a recurring autobiographical motif in his poetry, playing on the tension between piety and passion.

titleEn: Despair of People's Loyalty

verses.sadr: كم صاحب قد كان لي وامقا
أقول لو قد نال هذا الغنى ،
حتى إذا صار إلى ما اشتهى ،

translationEn: The poet counsels himself and the listener to despair of people's loyalty — for true wealth lies in that very despair. He recalls a companion who was devoted to him when that companion was bankrupt, and the poet thought: if this man ever gains wealth, he will honor me above all. But once the man attained his desires and was counted among the prosperous, he severed every bond of friendship with heaps of gold coins, not even content with a mere axe-blow's worth of severance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: despair, hopelessness (positively: liberation from dependence on others)
a great sum of gold, a large talent of money
the rope of friendship and purity
axe; here metaphor for a sharp, decisive break

context: A satirical poem about fair-weather friendship and the corrupting effect of wealth on loyalty. Abu Nuwas uses the personal experience of betrayal by a newly prosperous friend to make a universal observation about how prosperity changes people's relationships.

titleAr: ألا ليت شعري هكذا أنت للناس
titleEn: Are You Like This with Everyone?

verses.sadr: ألا ليت شعري هكذا أنت للناس ،
verses.ajuz: فأقدع عنك القلب يا صاح بالياس

verses.sadr: فقد كنت دهرا لا تروق لمعجب ،
verses.ajuz: سواي ، ولا تنمي إخائي إلى باس

verses.sadr: ولكنني لما بدا منك ما بدا ،
إذن ليس تزري بي لديك مودتي ،

translationEn: The poet cries out wishing he knew whether his beloved treated everyone the same way — for if so, he could restrain his heart through despair. For ages, the beloved seemed to please no admirer but him, and his fraternal affection seemed to lead nowhere troubling. But when the beloved's true nature revealed itself and he weighed his situation by a measuring scale, he understood: it is not his affection that diminishes him in the beloved's eyes, but rather his poverty that diminishes the beloved's love for him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: would that I knew, I wish I could know
to restrain, curb, hold back from
to please, delight, appeal to someone
a standard of measure, a scale or gauge
to belittle, diminish, bring shame upon

context: A compact ghazal blending romantic longing with bitter social observation, as Abu Nuwas realizes that his poverty — not his character — is the reason for the beloved's coldness toward him.

titleEn: Paper Merchants Who Cannot Spare a Sheet
translationEn: The poet requests a mere scrap of paper, yet his companions — most of whom are paper merchants by trade — cannot spare him one. May God curse their friendship and acquaintance, for their wealthy men are no different from their paupers when it comes to stinginess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a piece of paper, a writing sheet
papers, sheets; also paper merchants or dealers
may God curse them (imprecation of disgrace)
the wealthy, those of comfortable means

context: A sharp two-verse satirical epigram (نتفة) lamenting the stinginess of friends. The irony of paper merchants refusing to spare a scrap of paper is a biting piece of social comedy typical of Abu Nuwas's concise wit.

titleEn: The Good Men Are Gone, Leaving Wretches
translationEn: The true men have departed and risen high; we are left behind among the vilest of sub-human wretches. Every time the poet comes seeking benefit from them, they preempt his request with despair before he even asks. They weep before him until he wishes he could escape by bashing his head against theirs. You count them among the many, but when you examine them closely, they are not true men at all.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rose, departed, became fully independent and elevated
mythical sub-human creatures; used for vile or worthless beings
attainment, getting benefit or favor from someone
they rushed ahead of me, preempted me, got there first
with hopelessness, with despair as their answer
a multitude, a large enumerable number

context: A satirical poem (هجاء) lamenting the degeneracy of contemporary society, contrasting the noble men of the past with the contemptible ones who remain. The closing verse — 'they are not men at all' — delivers a devastating punch line.

titleEn: Describing a Keen Hunting Hound

verses.ajuz: من غير ما بيع ولا مكاس

verses.sadr: كم تيس رمل لاح في الكناس ،

translationEn: The poet describes a hunting dog trained on the drill — with the sunken temples of its skull exposed, rolling two eager, fiery eyes like glowing embers. Its eyes glow like the doe-eyed gazelle's, and its sleek body is like a branch of myrtle. What a fine companion and consoling brother it is — without price or bargaining. How many a desert mountain goat, glimpsed in the thicket, has it wrestled into the dust at the sides of the encampments. No one but Abu Nuwas has been given a dog like this.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I describe, I praise by detailed description
trained by beating on brass/copper (a training method)
the skull sutures, the bones of the head
the two eye sockets, the orbital hollows
myrtle tree, known for its slender branches
the one who consoles, who gives comfort in hardship

context: A poem of the hunting dog genre (وصف الكلب) within broader nature poetry. Abu Nuwas praises the hound's physical and hunting qualities with technical vocabulary, ending with a self-compliment that no one else was worthy of such a dog.

titleEn: How Are You, O Muhammad ibn Quraysh?

verses.ajuz: فيم ذا بل علام ذا أم لأيش ؟

verses.sadr: نحن في حان تاجر عندنا اللـه

translationEn: The poet greets his friend Muhammad ibn Quraysh, asking how his morning is, and playfully lamenting how his friend could justify abandoning him. They are in a merchant's tavern, sober in mind and measured in reason, not reckless. The wine brought from Tayzinabaadh is the pinnacle of all life's pleasures. He invites the friend to come drink with them at this early hour, expressing hope to one day see him commanding an army. He closes with a backhanded compliment: the friend's stinginess rivals the generosity of Ibn Hubaysh — a comic inversion.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may you never lose (a blessing formula)
my abandonment, throwing me aside, neglecting me
to drink the morning cup, to have a morning drink

context: A convivial poem (مجون) inviting a friend to join a wine session in a tavern, blending warm greeting, gentle reproach, and comic flattery. The reference to Tayzinabaadh (a district near Baghdad) grounds the poem in the Abbasid urban setting.

titleEn: A Languid Beloved Who Reproaches the Poet

verses.sadr: أقول له يوما ، وقد شفني الهوى :
verses.ajuz: أطلت عذابي فيك يا خير من نشا

verses.sadr: فقال : ألما يأن أن تترك الصبا
verses.ajuz: ومالك يا هذا ! وما لي ! وما تشا !

translationEn: A gazelle of a youth — languid and soft, effeminate in manner, the most beautiful of creatures and the most graceful of walkers. The poet tells him one day, his passion having worn him through: 'You have prolonged my torment, O finest of those who ever grew up.' The beloved replies sharply: 'Is it not yet time for you to abandon youthful folly? What is wrong with you? What do you want from me? Do whatever you wish!'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gazelle; used as a term of beauty for a beloved
languor, softness, a languid quality in the eyes
wore me through, consumed me, weakened me entirely
youthful folly, the frivolity of youth

context: A brief ghazal in which the beloved's withering reply — scolding the poet for not outgrowing his passion — turns the romantic plea into an unexpectedly comic and sharp exchange. The three-verse form captures a complete dramatic encounter.

titleEn: True Praise for the Caliph al-Amin

verses.sadr: قد ينقص القمر المنير ، إذا استوى

translationEn: The poet offers praise to Caliph al-Amin Muhammad — praise given without any expectation of commercial return. His praise of al-Amin is truthful, while other panegyrics are mere fabrications and empty invention. The full moon wanes when it reaches its peak, but the radiance of Muhammad's face does not diminish. And when the Abbasids are counted bead by bead, Muhammad is their refined and extracted ruby.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Trustworthy (title of Caliph Muhammad al-Amin)
waiting in anticipation, watching for an opportunity (here: seeking reward)
fabrication, making things up, baseless invention
the refined ruby, the purified gemstone

context: A panegyric addressed to the Abbasid Caliph Muhammad al-Amin (r. 809-813 CE), one of Abu Nuwas's key patrons. The poet distinguishes his sincere praise from the flattery of court poets who seek reward, positioning himself as offering genuine admiration.

titleEn: A Message of Harsh Reproach for Hamdan
translationEn: Tell Hamdan — though sincere counsel is not in my nature — that he is neither a free man to be blamed and corrected, nor a slave to be disciplined with the rod. May God's mercy be upon Adam — a mercy both universal and particular — for had Adam known that someone like Hamdan would descend among his children, he would have castrated himself to prevent his lineage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be blamed, reproached, held accountable
I seek to appease or discipline him
encompassed all, was general and universal
particularized, showed special favor to individuals

context: A devastating satirical poem (هجاء) addressed to a person named Hamdan. The closing verse — wishing Adam had castrated himself to prevent Hamdan's birth — is among the most extreme examples of Abu Nuwas's caustic satirical wit.

titleEn: Gazelles of the Royal Court

verses.sadr: إذا ما أوتر الموتر منهم عجل النبضا
verses.ajuz: وإن أقرض ذا هذا نوالا عجل النبضا

translationEn: In the royal chancery are gazelles — their eyes languid with a sickly beauty — bred in the palaces of eternity, knowing no sleeplessness, never accustomed to crouching in the open wastelands, never having left the comforts and ease of life since they came to be. They refer all matters to a glittering-eyed master who has already resolved them — an imam who is tyrannical, harsh, and whose word becomes the accepted ruling. When one of them is agitated, his pulse races; when one lends another a gift, the pulse races as well. Were it not that fish devour each other, O calamity, they would have filled the whole earth with their teeming numbers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gazelles; used metaphorically for beautiful courtiers
those raised under protection, foster-daughters of palaces
eternity, paradise; here used for permanent palace residence
sleeplessness, the inability to close one's eyes
the open desolate wasteland, flat featureless desert
one with strikingly beautiful eyes, with intensely dark irises

context: A complex satirical ghazal targeting effete courtiers at the royal chancery, using the gazelle metaphor ironically. The poem blends erotic imagery, political satire, and a wry observation about the natural order — all fish eat each other — as a commentary on court survival.

titleEn: O You Who Turn Away, My Soul Is Your Ransom
translationEn: O you who turn away — my soul is your ransom, say that to one who turns away from you in return. Has my love for you, my master, dissolved so quickly? You hate me, my lord, yet I ransom you with a love that is itself hated. I remain as one fasting through your anger, waiting for your contentment to break my fast. How strange is the one who blames the lover — has he not himself both loved and hated? Does he see the path of these two emotions as one already traveled? Or was he simply indifferent, knowing neither, and thus finished? I have yearning while he has forgetfulness — while I stay awake, he sleeps.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who turns away, averts his face
my soul is your ransom (expression of extreme devotion)
fasting through your anger (metaphor of patient endurance)
your contentment will break my fast (beautiful metaphor)
forgetfulness, consolation, moving on from grief

context: A tender ghazal in which the poet addresses a beloved who has turned away, using the extended metaphor of fasting and breaking fast to describe the pain of estrangement and the hope of reconciliation.

titleEn: When You Were Beardless Your Beauty Shone
translationEn: Why did you not yield then, when your face was radiant in the freshness of youth, with barely a hair on your cheeks? Now that a beard has grown upon your face, it has taken away your charm — enough to fill a grasping hand. You are like fine wine that has been left: after all the sweetness, the pressed grape has turned into sharp vinegar.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the water of the face; its radiance and luster
the first budding of youth, the onset of adolescence
the cheek, the side of the face where facial hair first grows
wine vinegar, sour vinegar made from wine

context: A brief ghazal lamenting the end of youthful beauty with the growth of the beard. The wine-turned-vinegar simile is a classic literary image that Abu Nuwas deploys with characteristic economy and precision.

titleEn: The Yolk Is Gone and Only the Membrane Remains
translationEn: The yolk has gone, and time has left nothing but the membrane and the empty shell. Goodness and kindness will not return until a chick hatches from under an elephant's egg — a thing that cannot be. Yet perhaps God will open up a basin of generosity and good deeds anew.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the egg yolk; metaphor for the best and richest part
the thin membrane inside an eggshell
the empty eggshell after the chick has hatched

context: A three-verse poem of resigned despair about the disappearance of generosity and goodness in the poet's world, expressed through a striking egg metaphor. The final verse pivots to a quiet hope in God's ability to restore what has been lost.

titleEn: O Beloved Whose Illness Worsened My Heart
translationEn: O beloved who is ill — your illness has increased the sickness of my heart, and this was done against my will, not by my consent. May the Most Merciful turn harm away from you and onto me; let my own soul be the captive that bears the worst of fate's decrees. What does relentless time want from me, curse it — I had barely stopped fearing time before it rose to intercept me again.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: against my will, in spite of me
with my soul as a hostage or shackle
the worst of what fate decrees
curse it, woe to it (exclamation of distress)
stood in the way, intercepted, rose against

context: A brief but emotionally dense ghazal addressed to a sick beloved, where the poet offers himself as a substitute for the beloved's suffering. The final verse widens the lens to a lament about the cruelty of fate in general.

titleEn: O You Who Embody Pure Beauty
translationEn: O you who embody pure and unmixed beauty, who sway like a fresh and supple branch — if my life were to make you angry, I would kill myself just to earn your contentment.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fresh and supple, newly grown (of a branch)
made you angry, provoked your wrath
so that you might be pleased, to earn your contentment

context: A two-verse love epigram of extreme devotion. The hyperbolic willingness to die for the beloved's contentment is a conventional ghazal motif, expressed here with characteristic Abu Nuwas economy — all meaning compressed into two verses.

titleEn: Drink Without Restraint, God Forgives
translationEn: Abandon restraint in drinking, and take the cup with eagerness — drink from the chestnut-brown wine that flashes like a Persian lightning bolt, glowing inside the large drinking vessels. Do not hold back, for God's forgiveness will be freely dispensed tomorrow at the Bridge of the Straight Path. Forgiveness was created for none other than the sinning human being.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: falling short, holding back, restraining oneself
like the lightning of Persia (poetic expression for brilliant flash)
large drinking bowls, wide wine cups
the Bridge (on the Day of Judgment in Islamic eschatology)

context: A characteristic Abu Nuwas khamriyyah that audaciously uses Islamic eschatological concepts — God's forgiveness at the Sirat bridge — to justify drinking. This theological playfulness is one of his most controversial and distinctive rhetorical moves.

titleEn: Love Broke My Vitality
translationEn: Love has broken the poet's vitality — and he had been full of energy. A message reached him about the beloved that increased his despair. He cries out in loss: is someone like him expected to find a companion among mixed company? He resolves to associate only with the family of Amr or with Luqayt. He observes how many Arab women have maintained intimate relationships with Nabataean men. Had the beloved wanted union, the poet says, they would not have extracted such conditions of modesty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a mixed companion, one of mixed origin
the Nabataeans, non-Arab rural inhabitants of Iraq
union, connection, intimacy with the beloved

context: A ghazal of frustrated love that takes an unexpected turn into social commentary on inter-ethnic relationships in Abbasid Iraq, using the Nabataeans as a foil to argue that tribal and ethnic pride is no obstacle to love when the desire is genuine.

titleEn: A Beautiful Youth by the Nile
translationEn: A youth of wondrous creation, full in his features, slender at the waist like a tethered mare — his father is from the great Copts of Egypt, a lineage far above the Nabataeans. He gave the poet the clear water of the Nile to drink at ease, along with an expansive wine from the vineyards of the villages of Asyut — wine with two qualities of taste and scent, and a color in the glass like sesame oil. They were alone together: the poet contending with him over the wine cup and reciting to him poems in the Basit meter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: full in his facial lines and features
tethered, a tethered horse (implying slender, controlled grace)
Copts, the indigenous Christian inhabitants of Egypt
a wide, expansive wine; also pure wine
sesame oil (used to describe the wine's golden color)
wine (specifically wine drunk communally, a type)

context: A khamriyyah set in Egypt, combining the poet's praise of a Coptic youth with a description of Egyptian wine from the vineyards of Asyut. The poem reflects Abu Nuwas's travels and his characteristic interweaving of erotic admiration with wine description.

titleEn: I Traded Vitality for Brokenness Through Love
translationEn: The poet has exchanged vitality for brokenness, and love has bound him with affliction's ropes. Were it not that he wields patience over his heart, it would burst from its sinews. A fool told him: 'If only you would stop!' The poet replied: 'Our meeting will be on the Bridge of Judgment.' Had the fool not made himself a guest on his carpet when he rebuked him, the poet would have shown him reason enough to excuse those who fall for the passion of full-figured houris. Your blame of me, and your saying 'leave him' — these fall harder on me than the lash of whips.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: brokenness, a fracturing of spirit and vitality
the sinews attaching the heart, the pericardium
a fool, a stupid person (strong insult)
the Bridge over Hell (on the Day of Judgment)
the full-figured houris, the women of paradise with full shapes

context: A ghazal of stubborn passion, where Abu Nuwas dismisses the reproacher's counsel with characteristic defiance, using an eschatological metaphor — 'we'll meet on the Bridge of Judgment' — to underscore his unyielding attachment to his beloved.

titleEn: The Hunting Dog Tears Through Fresh Prey
translationEn: The hunting dog tears through the prey when the quarry is fresh — with claws dark as the soot of cooking stones smeared with grease. It pricks up its ears in alertness, so that the two hollows of its skull resemble parallel slits. The hounds rush like something snatched up instantly, faster than a sandgrouse can cry its name. They prey upon the spotted desert gazelles, meeting from this dog an inescapably harsh judge — shattering bones and tearing fresh hides like craftsmen cutting fine cloth, when blood mingled with dust turns grey-white. Praise be to God for what He has bestowed!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the three cooking stones of a hearth
smeared, covered in grease or soot
the sandgrouse's cry of its own name (قطا); a proverbial expression of speed)
blood, especially fresh blood of game

context: A hunting poem (طرد) describing a dog's ferocious hunting prowess in vivid, technical detail. Abu Nuwas closes with a pious praise of God for the gift of such a hunting companion, a conventional closing move that frames the secular hunt within a devotional frame.

titleEn: A Fierce Dog Prepared for the Hunt
translationEn: The poet has prepared a fierce dog for the chase — when it sets out in the morning from hunger it blazes with intensity. It pulls at the leash and insists stubbornly, as though a devil has been set upon it to urge it on. It presses flock after flock of gazelles relentlessly until you see them shattering apart in terror. Every day it wins its share of the hunt until you see the blood of the prey pooling and spreading.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the pursuit hunt, chasing game on horseback or by dogs
gluttony, intense hunger, greed for food
blazed, burned, flamed (with intensity or hunger)
spread out, pooled, pressed onto the ground

context: A compact six-verse hunting poem (طرد) describing a hunting dog's ferocious energy from the moment it is unleashed through its successful daily hunt. The demonic simile — 'as though a devil has been set upon it' — vividly captures the dog's uncontrollable drive.

titleAr: ما مثل هذا اليوم في طيبه
titleEn: No Day Finer Than This for Wine

verses.sadr: ما مثل هذا اليوم في طيبه

verses.ajuz: تحب في ذا اليوم أن نصنعا ؟ !

verses.sadr: هل لك أن تغدو على قهوة

translationEn: No day is as fine as this in its sweetness — it has not been wasted without amusement nor spent idly. What do you think of it? What would you like us to do on this day? How about starting the morning with a cup of coffee-like wine that courses quickly through a man when he downs it quickly? People have never found, nor have they ever experienced, anything that drives away sorrow as effectively as it does.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its goodness, its pleasantness, its fine quality
spent without use, left idle, wasted
wine (an early meaning of قهوة before it came to mean coffee)
moves quickly through, acts swiftly within
that which drives away, a repellent of sorrow

context: A convivial invitation poem that captures a perfect day and invites a companion to spend it drinking. Notable for its use of قهوة in its original sense of wine — long before the word came to denote coffee in Arabic.

titleEn: I Have Abandoned Folly on the Caliph's Command

verses.ajuz: هي اليوم حرب ، وهي أمس شياع

translationEn: O reproacher, I have sold off my foolishness in the market where it is traded, and bared my head with nothing left to veil it. The Commander of the Faithful has forbidden me from youthful abandon, and the Commander of the Faithful's command is to be obeyed. A pleasure I have abandoned out of the Imam's reproof — though it held for the pleasure-seeker both spectacle and sound. He who is saturated with the water of youth seems to thirst and hunger from the tightening of his belly. I have restricted my soul from wine — which today is forbidden, though yesterday it was freely shared.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the reproacher, the one who blames and rebukes
youthful folly, the frivolity of youth
one whose command is obeyed, a followed authority
the tightening and thinning of the belly (from abstinence)
freely shared, spread widely, open to all

context: A poem of ostensible repentance and compliance with the Caliph's prohibition on the poet's wine-drinking and youthful behavior. The ambiguous, almost ironic tone — describing the pleasures he 'gave up' in loving detail — leaves open whether Abu Nuwas is sincere or merely performing compliance.

titleEn: Pour Me Seven in Succession
translationEn: Pour me seven in succession, and pass them around quickly — a wine that the onlooker thinks has scattered into beams of light if it spills. O my two friends, drink it and cast aside all restraint. The blamer came to me early with reproach, and did his worst to dissuade me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine (classical usage); also coffee in modern Arabic
to come early, to do something at the crack of dawn

context: A brief but vivid khamriyyat in the mujazza' al-ramal meter. Abu Nuwas opens with the bold command to be served seven cups in rapid succession — a typical gesture of libertine excess. The image of wine scattering as rays of light is a classic Abu Nuwas trope elevating wine to an almost sacred luminosity. The blamer arrives at dawn but is powerless against the poet's resolve.

titleEn: Would That the Omen-Reader's Rebuke Were Here
translationEn: Would that the augury of the omen-reader had been present with me, when I was confused between her written sign and the rising star. She sealed her complaint to me with a seal engraved: 'Abandonment is swift and my estrangement is my cure.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the omen-reader, the woman who reads auguries

context: A short, cryptic ghazal in the kamil meter. Abu Nuwas employs the imagery of omen-reading and celestial signs to express romantic confusion. The beloved seals a farewell with a ring-impression that declares her departure is both swift and curative — a bitter irony the poet savors.

titleEn: Three Who Ruled Kings, Each Incomparable
translationEn: Three men ruled over kings — when assessed, each was a radiant, awe-inspiring figure. Al-Rabi' ruled, and al-Fadl ruled after him, and through the noble al-Abbas the branches rose high. Abbas is Abbas when the heat of battle flares; and al-Fadl is al-Fadl, and al-Rabi' is al-Rabi'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: branches (metaphor for offspring and lineage rising high)

context: A concise panegyric praising three prominent Abbasid courtiers: al-Rabi' ibn Yunus, al-Fadl ibn al-Rabi', and al-Abbas ibn al-Fadl. The closing line's triple repetition — each man named twice — is a rhetorical device Abu Nuwas uses to assert that each of these men is so individually defined by his excellence that his name alone suffices as his praise.

titleEn: Had My Fortune Not Been Ill
translationEn: Had my fortune not been so wretched, Musa would not have died so quickly, nor would fate have folded him away before I could see all the Barmakids — whom God decreed would hang like crucified trunks on the banks of the Tigris. This is the age of apes, so bow down and be obedient and submissive to them. It is as if what overtook Ya'qub and al-Rabi' has now come for them too.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: selected them for punishment, castrated them (figurative: humiliated)
tree trunks; the crucified (those hung on poles)
apes, monkeys (a term of contempt for the foolish or base)

context: A sharp satirical lament over the fall of the Barmakids — the powerful Persian family that served as viziers to Harun al-Rashid until their dramatic downfall in 803 CE. Abu Nuwas mourns his patron Musa ibn Yahya al-Barmaki and mockingly dubs the new era 'the age of apes.' The reference to crucifixion on the banks of the Tigris alludes to the public execution of Ja'far al-Barmaki. The poem drips with bitter irony.

titleEn: I Woke the Hungriest of All God's Creation

verses.ajuz: وأفزع الناس من خبز ، إذا وضعا
بارك الله في ضيف إذا شبعا
فقد ترون بحلقي اليوم ما صنعا

translationEn: I woke up the hungriest of all God's creatures, and the most terrified of people by bread when it is placed before them. Al-Mufaddal's bread has written upon it: 'God bless the guest when he is full!' I warn you against our companion's bread — you can see by my throat today what it has done.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the most frightened, the most alarmed
I warn you, I caution you

context: A comic hija' poem mocking al-Mufaddal, a stingy host whose bread is so hard or so meager that it injures the poet's throat. The joke about bread 'inscribed' with a blessing for the already-full guest is a sarcastic twist on the Arabic custom of generosity. Abu Nuwas excels at this kind of sharp domestic satire.

titleEn: Tell Isma'il of the Mole
translationEn: Tell Isma'il of the mole on the wide cheek, he of the skull that protrudes like a shin-bone, he of the mouth that gapes wide at the jaw — nine spans across — and he of the pain below, arm upon arm entwined: your wedding was a feast for hungry hawks. Cups circled among you in song and listening; you divided yourselves in the darkness like sheep among predators. A night in which Iblis rejoiced at your gathering — camels were ridden until the caller for dawn arose.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the mole (beauty mark on the face)
the skull, the top of the head
the leg or shin of an animal; also a rocky outcrop
the jaw, the corner of the mouth

context: A scurrilous hija' against a man named Isma'il and his wedding night. Abu Nuwas systematically mocks the man's physical ugliness and the debauchery of the wedding gathering. The poem is a characteristic example of the classical Arabic hija' (lampoon) genre at its most biting and grotesque.

titleAr: ما رعى الدهر آل برمك حقا
titleEn: Time Did Not Honor the Barmakids

verses.sadr: ما رعى الدهر آل برمك حقا ،
إن دهرا لم يرع حقا ليحيى ،

translationEn: Time did not honor the Barmakids justly when it struck their rule with something terrible. A time that did not honor its covenant with Yahya will certainly not honor the covenant of the family of al-Rabi'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to honor, to keep faith with, to guard
Yahya ibn Khalid al-Barmaki, the Barmakid patriarch
the family of al-Rabi' ibn Yunus, Abbasid courtiers

context: A two-verse elegy on the fall of the Barmakids. Abu Nuwas extends his lament into a political warning: if time could betray the great Yahya al-Barmaki, then the family of al-Rabi' — another powerful Abbasid clan — is equally vulnerable. The poem is a statement of disillusionment with the fickleness of political fortune.

titleEn: My Father as Ransom for One Who Visited Me

verses.ajuz: في شهر ذي الحجة من نصفه

translationEn: My father as ransom for the one who came to visit me in the middle of the month of Dhul-Hijja. He spent the night offering me from his cheek a wine through his eyes, and wine from his hand. And between all that, I sometimes drew his anklets close to his earring.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my father as ransom (an expression of endearment)
he offered me, he passed to me (wine or a cup)
Dhul-Hijja, the twelfth Islamic month (pilgrimage month)

context: A delicate ghazal describing a secret visit from a beloved during the sacred month of pilgrimage. The image of drinking 'wine from his eyes' is a classic Abu Nuwas metaphor: the beloved's glance intoxicates. The final verse — drawing the beloved's anklets up to his earring — is an erotic metaphor of suppleness and intimacy delivered with characteristic indirection.

titleEn: Pour for Me and Pour for Dhafafah
translationEn: Pour for me and pour for Dhafafah, O Abu al-Hurr, its pure first-pressing. And pour for the head of pleasure and elegance, with the good omen of the augurer. A wine of swaggering grace, free of every flaw. Others abandon it only out of hope or fear. Bring it openly, and spare me tales of nonsense. Lost — rather, humiliated — is he who shows violence against it, O Dhafafah. Just as the caliphate was humiliated and wasted after Harun.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Dhafafah, a companion or cupbearer's name
the first pressing of wine, the purest portion
nonsense, fable (also a proper name in folklore)

context: A lively khamriyyat in mujazza' al-ramal addressed to Dhafafah, a drinking companion.
