The poem takes a sharp political turn in its final verse: Abu Nuwas compares the degradation of the caliphate after Harun al-Rashid to the humiliation of anyone who dares to refuse wine. This bold political metaphor — wine as civilization — is a hallmark of his most daring poetry.

titleEn: Obey the Caliph, Defy the Musician
translationEn: Obey the Caliph, defy the musician, and keep away from pleasure and revelry. The Caliph's eye is fixed upon me — his glance has tied the knot of wariness around mine. My public conduct toward him is sound, but I see that the religion of my inner self teeters on an edge. If I promised you that I would abandon wine, I would fear for you my own broken vow. The cup circled and its blows fell — and his eye is made to feign the opposite of what it hides. And a wine by which souls come alive, whose glorious qualities surpass all description! It had aged in its jar for an age, until it diminished by half. They pulled away the clay mask from a barely living thing, on the very brink of death. It breathed into the room when mixed, like basil breathing into the nostril. From the hand of a bejeweled cupbearer — what beauty, what elegance! She looked with the eyes of a lost young gazelle, and turned with the graceful neck of a fawn. She said, swaying toward me like one walking on a tambourine: 'My face, when I come forward, intercedes for me — and the torment of your heart is the beauty of what lies behind me.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its jar, its barrel (the wine vessel)
the last breath of life, a faint remainder
a young gazelle or calf (symbol of soft beauty)
sides of the neck (a symbol of gracefulness)

context: A masterful khamriyyat in which Abu Nuwas ironically pretends to comply with the Caliph's disapproval of wine, only to launch into one of his most lyrical wine descriptions. The poem moves from political calculation to a vivid portrait of an aged vintage, then to an enchanting female cupbearer. The closing verses, in which the cupbearer speaks of her own beauty, are among the most quoted in Abu Nuwas's divan.

titleAr: لو كان حي وائلا من التلف
titleEn: If Any Living Thing Could Escape Perdition

verses.sadr: لو كان حي وائلا من التلف ،

verses.ajuz: هاتيك ، أو عصماء في أعلى شرف
أودى جماع العلم مذ أودى خلف

verses.sadr: من لا يعد العلم إلا ما عرف ،

translationEn: If any living thing could escape perdition, then a hawk perched on the highest rocky summit — a mother of a chick she has sheltered in a cleft, its throat still downy, not yet eating with a claw — like one sitting bent from senility. Or a mountain ibex at the peak of a crag, evading the familiar huntsman's snares and draws. The sum of knowledge has perished since Khalaf perished — he who counted knowledge only as what he himself knew, a vast ocean among the mighty seas. From him we would draw at will, a transmission not to be gathered from written scrolls.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a cleft, a hollow in a rock
downy, covered in fine feathers (of a chick)

context: An elegy for Khalaf al-Ahmar, the famous grammarian and transmitter of poetry who died around 796 CE. Abu Nuwas opens with an elaborate image of wild creatures — hawk and ibex — at the peaks of existence, none escaping death. The poem is a tribute to oral transmission over written learning, reflecting the values of the classical Arabic scholarly tradition.

titleAr: شعر ميت أتاك في لفظ حي
titleEn: Dead Poetry Came to You in Living Words

verses.sadr: شعر ميت أتاك في لفظ حي ،

verses.ajuz: لم تبن من كتاب وجهي حرفا

translationEn: Dead poetry came to you in living words, suspended between life and death. Calamities have so wasted its body that it almost disappears from the eyes of calamity itself. If you were to look at me carefully to confirm my face, you would not make out a single letter from the writing of my face. And you would cast your glance again at one whom illness has so eroded that he is nearly effaced.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: calamities, misfortunes, the blows of fate
you contemplated me, you gazed carefully at me
you could not make out, you could not discern

context: A poem of extreme physical and poetic abasement. Abu Nuwas presents himself — or his verse — as a dying thing barely visible to the eye. The opening paradox of 'dead poetry in living words' is a meditation on the relationship between the suffering poet and his art. Whether this reflects genuine illness or is a rhetorical posture of humility before a patron is debated by scholars.

titleEn: A Blamer Rebuked Me, Bringing Innovation

verses.sadr: لحاني كي لا أشرب الراح ، إنها
هي الشمس إلا أن للشمس وقدة ،
فنحن ، وإن لم نسكن الخلد عاجلا ،
verses.ajuz: فما خلدنا في الدهر إلا رحيقها

verses.sadr: فيا أيها اللاحي اسقني ثم غنني ،
إذا مت فادفني إلى جنب كرمة ،

translationEn: A blamer rebuked me, wishing to introduce an innovation — that, by my life, is a path I cannot bear. He rebuked me so I would not drink wine, claiming it leaves a heavy burden of sin on whoever tastes it. But the blamers only increased my stubbornness in clinging to it, for as long as I live I am its companion. Shall I abandon it? By God, even He did not abandon its name — and the Commander of the Faithful himself is its friend. It is the sun, except that the sun burns — and our wine surpasses it in every beauty. Though we may not dwell in paradise soon, our immortality in this world is nothing but its nectar. O blamer, pour for me then sing to me — for I am its twin brother until the moment of death. When I die, bury me beside a grapevine whose roots will water my bones after my death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: innovation, novelty (often in a negative religious sense)
its pure nectar (riqhiq — a term for the finest wine, used in the Quran for the drink of Paradise)

context: One of Abu Nuwas's most celebrated khamriyyat, this poem is a sustained and witty defense of wine-drinking against religious critics. He invokes the Caliph's own friendship with wine, elevates it above the sun, and closes with the unforgettable wish to be buried beside a grapevine. The use of 'raqhiq' — the Quranic word for Paradise's wine — is deliberately provocative. The poem encapsulates his entire philosophy of libertine aesthetics.

titleEn: O Blamer of the Lover, You Are the One
translationEn: O blamer of the lover — you are the one who blames all who love and all who adore. May I be a ransom for one whose glance spoke to me in secret, away from people and from any tongue. He signaled with his eyes a greeting, while his heart was fluttering from fear. I went away joyful at what I had attained, yet my heart held a burning ember within it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may I be a ransom for (an expression of deep affection)

context: A tender ghazal capturing the stolen moment of a secret glance between two lovers in public. Abu Nuwas describes the beloved's fearful heartbeat, the silent greeting communicated only through the eyes, and his own bittersweet joy at receiving it. The concluding image of a burning ember inside the joyful heart is a classic Arabic image for the pain that underlies romantic happiness.

titleEn: I Have Died Save for the Last Breath
translationEn: I have died save for the last thin breath of life, from love of a kohl-eyed graceful youth of charm. His waist is slender and tucked in, while what rises above from waist and belt is full and swelling. His beauty captivates in all its features, between what is joined and what is separate. No horizon of his stature is singled out as superior to another. And when he appears, his beauties forcibly drag toward him the reins of every gazing eye.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the faint last breath, the barest flicker of life
a fawn (metaphor for a graceful youth)
one of natural grace, a noble youth
slenderness of the belly, a tucked-in waist
the reins of gazing eyes (a metaphor for compelled attention)

context: A ghazal of erotic devotion structured around the detailed praise of a youth's physical beauty — the classical Arabic genre of wasf al-ghulam. Abu Nuwas describes the body from waist outward, using the metaphor of horizons to suggest that every part of the beloved's figure is equally perfect. The closing image of eyes being dragged like bridled horses toward him is vivid and characteristic.

titleEn: O You Who Direct My Words to Their Ugliest

verses.sadr: لو كان من قال نارا أحرقت فمه

translationEn: O you who direct my words toward their ugliest utterance — because he is a sorcerer of the eyes, a beloved. If the one who said 'fire' had been burned by it in the mouth, no creature would ever have spoken the word 'fire.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sorcerer of the eyes, one whose eyes bewitch
to utter, to pronounce a word

context: A brief but philosophically resonant two-verse poem. The first verse accuses the beloved of causing the poet to say ugly things — presumably the explicit erotic speech that fills his poetry. The second verse is a celebrated philosophical aphorism: if speaking words caused their effect, no one would dare name fire. Abu Nuwas uses this to defend the freedom of poetic speech.

titleEn: My Blamer in Wine, I Will Not Please You

verses.sadr: لا تسم المدام ، إن لمت فيها ،

translationEn: O my blamer in wine, I will not please you — it is foolish to blame one who defies you. Do not name the wine when you rebuke it, lest you defile its beautiful name with your mouth. Pour for us, O our two cupbearers, a vintage ten years old in which you would imagine there is a bar of gold. And when water is mixed with it, you would think pearls are strung upon pearls within it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a bar of gold, a precious ingot
strung, threaded (like pearls on a string)

context: A khamriyyat in the khafif meter, addressed with mock exasperation to a wine-blamer. The central image — wine mixed with water producing the appearance of pearls upon pearls — is a famous Abu Nuwas wine metaphor. The command not to even name the wine so as not to defile its beautiful name with the blamer's unworthy mouth is a witty inversion of religious censure.

titleEn: O Month, How Long Will You Last?
translationEn: O month — how long will you stay? We have grown sick and weary of you. Whenever Shawwal is praised, we denounce you. Would that you had departed — though we have no hope of that. And if it were possible to kill a month, we would kill you!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you remain, how long will you last
we have grown bored of you, we are weary of you
Shawwal, the tenth Islamic month (following Ramadan)
we reviled you, we condemned you

context: A short comic poem addressed to the month of Ramadan, which Abu Nuwas addresses directly with mounting exasperation. The month of fasting forbids wine, and the poet — with characteristic audacity — addresses Ramadan as though it were a tiresome houseguest who has overstayed his welcome. The comic climax, imagining the murder of a month, is quintessential Abu Nuwas humor.

titleEn: When Separation Is Mentioned, He Weeps

verses.sadr: رأى ما بي فقال : من ال

translationEn: When separation is mentioned he weeps, and when the watcher is inattentive he complains. Your image is set before his eyes — he sees it wherever he walks. He saw my state and said: 'Who is it that has burned you with blame?' 'Tell me, for whom is all this suffering, so I may reproach him?' I said: 'For you.' He turned away and did not speak to me — such is the master when he holds power.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the watcher, the guard, the jealous observer
set before his eyes, fixed in his sight
the master, the lord, the patron

context: A ghazal of tender self-exposure. The poem describes the lover's state in the third person before shifting to direct address. The devastating reversal at the end — the beloved turns away and becomes cold the moment he is named — captures the power dynamic of the Arabic ghazal tradition, where the beloved's power increases the more the lover confesses.

titleEn: If the One You Love Loved You in Return

verses.sadr: هل غير أن كنت فتى عاشقا ،

translationEn: If the one you love loved you in return, your world would be settled in sweet contentment. Far from it! This is a wish you have — one you fed to your heart, and it fed you. What do you hope for, while love keeps striking the flint of your mortal fate? You planted the branch of love, and when it bore fruit, abandonment became your harvest. Would that I knew what love has done — and what that even is. Only that you were a young lover, and love destroyed you and led you astray. Its caller called you, and you answered, and you came running — but your endeavor was in vain! You complain but find no compassionate one, and find no one to answer your complaint. It is as if the one to whom you complain of love is deaf and cannot hear your whisper.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you love him, you yearn for him
far from it! impossible! (exclamation of impossibility)
a wish, a desire, a hope

context: A ghazal of resigned despair, addressed to the poet himself or to an imagined lover. Abu Nuwas traces the full arc of unrequited love: the impossible wish, the persistent pursuit, the bitter harvest of abandonment, and the final deafness of the beloved. The poem is structurally straightforward — accessible, direct, melancholic — and has been widely read as a statement on the hopelessness of romantic desire.

titleEn: You Threatened Me with Death Without Cause

verses.sadr: يا موعدي بالقتل قد حالف ال
يا من دعا قلبي إلى حبه ،

translationEn: You threatened me with death for no crime, while my heart is held captive in your two hands. O you who promise me death — the dagger has sworn by your two right hands to kill me. O you who called my heart to love you, and I said: 'Here I am, and twice at your service.' No dagger that would take my soul from me is more lethal than the languid droop of your two eyes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you threatened me, you warned me
held in pledge by your two hands, captive to you
has sworn an oath, has pledged
here I am and doubly at your service (a Hajj formula used playfully)
languidness, drooping (of the eyes — a sign of beauty)

context: A playful ghazal of mock-surrender before the beloved's 'murderous' beauty. Abu Nuwas uses the conceit of the beloved threatening death to structure a love poem, culminating in the classic reversal: the beloved's languid eyes are more lethal than any blade. The use of the Hajj formula 'labbayk wa sa'dayk' in a love context is a bold religious appropriation characteristic of Abu Nuwas.

titleEn: The Water of Youth Flows in Your Cheeks

verses.ajuz: لم يهن ما لقيت منك عليك

translationEn: The water of youth flows in your cheeks, and radiance shines upon your temples. Your kohl-lined glance cast its sorcery at my heart, and it became a pledge in your keeping. I am recklessly consumed in my love for you — I do not complain of my passion except to you yourself. O wondrous one of beauty and grace — the evidence: my life and death are in your two hands. My father as ransom for you — had you been afflicted with such longing, what I have suffered from you would not have weighed lightly upon you. By love, the arrows of death have become aimed at me, from your two eyes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two temples, the sides of the face

context: A ghazal of ardent devotion structured around the visual beauty of the beloved's face — cheeks, temples, kohl-lined eyes. Abu Nuwas escalates from physical description to existential declaration: life and death are in the beloved's hands. The closing verse, framing the beloved's eyes as arrows of death aimed at the poet, is a common but here unusually direct expression of the erotic-mortuary metaphor in Arabic ghazal.

titleEn: Our God, How Just You Are
translationEn: Our God, how just You are — King of all who have ever ruled. Here I am, I have answered Your call. Act and hasten before your appointed time. The dominion is Yours alone, with no partner. Never has a servant who asked You been disappointed. You are with him wherever he walks — were it not for You, O Lord, he would perish. Here I am! All praise is Yours, and the dominion, with no partner. Every prophet and king, and every one worthy of You, every servant who asked You — who glorified or answered the call — for You it is. Here I am! All praise is Yours, and the dominion, with no partner. By the night when it descends, and by the swimmers in the celestial sphere, along the courses of the orbiting bodies. Here I am! All praise is Yours, and the dominion, with no partner. End your deeds with good.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: here I am (the Hajj talbiyya formula of response to God)
your appointed term, your time of death
those swimming in the celestial sphere — stars or angels
the orbiting path, the celestial course

context: A devotional poem (zuhd) in which Abu Nuwas adapts the language of the Hajj talbiyya — 'Labbayk Allahumma labbayk' — into a poem of praise and repentance. The refrain 'lbabbayk inna al-hamda laka wal-mulk la sharika lak' is taken almost verbatim from the traditional pilgrimage formula. This is one of Abu Nuwas's tawbat (repentance) poems, demonstrating his capacity to shift from libertine verse to sincere religious expression.

titleEn: My Friends, By God, Do Not Dig
translationEn: My two friends, by God, do not dig my grave except at Qartabul, amid the wine-presses between the vineyards — and do not bring me near the wheat. Perhaps in my grave I will hear, when the grapes are pressed, the clamor of the treading feet.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Qartabul, a place known for its wine-presses near Baghdad
wheat, grain stalk (water is given to the water-drinkers)

context: One of Abu Nuwas's most beloved short poems, a comic last will and testament instructing his friends to bury him at Qartabul — a famous wine-producing district — so he can hear the wine-presses at work even in the grave. The rejection of burial near wheat (the symbol of abstinent sobriety) is a final swipe at the wine-shunners. The poem is pure Abu Nuwas: witty, irreverent, and utterly committed to wine even in death.

titleEn: Youth Was the Mount of Ignorance
translationEn: Youth was the mount of ignorance, the beautifier of laughter and levity. It was my beauty when I donned it and walked — the sandal's whisper was my fame. It was my eloquence when I spoke, and ears inclined to what was recited. It was my intercessor in my desires with the girl, and the one who gained my remedy. The one who roused me while people slept, until I became a husband's substitute. The one who commanded me — until when my spirit resolved, my hand was aided in the deed. Now I have turned to moderation, and I have unloaded youth's saddle from my back. Yet I still love the cup, even if it has taken from my livelihood and reduced my surplus. A golden one — its glory is in its overseers — surpassing all peers and comparisons. It was stored for Adam before his creation, so it preceded him by the step of what came before. It comes to you as something you cannot touch except with the sense of innate reason. The eye explores within it a clear face, pure of page, bright and smooth. And when water rises over it, it clothes it in bubbles like the anklet-bells of the partridge.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a riding mount, a vehicle (metaphor for what youth carries you toward)
its overseers, its noble administrators (Persian loanword: marzban)
was stored, was reserved, was set aside
the innate faculty of reason, instinctive intelligence
pure of face/page, clear and unclouded
the jingling anklet-bells of the partridge

context: A mature reflection on youth's passing, structured as a series of 'youth was...' clauses that praise what youth enabled — beauty, eloquence, erotic success — before accepting that age has now brought moderation. But Abu Nuwas does not fully abandon pleasure: the poem pivots to a long description of aged wine that surpasses even Adam in antiquity. The wine description ending with the image of bubbles like partridge-bells is one of his most beautiful.

titleEn: By Your Life, al-Amin Muhammad Was Not Absent

verses.ajuz: عن الأمر يعنيه ، إذا شهد الفضل
له دونه ما كان بينهما فضل
كما السهم فيه الريش والفوق والنصل

translationEn: By your life, al-Amin Muhammad was not absent from any matter that concerned him, whenever al-Fadl was present. Were it not that the inheritance of the caliphate belonged to him rather than al-Fadl, there would be no distinction between the two. Though their persons may differ, their word is one word and their deed is one deed. I see al-Fadl as one who unites this world and religion, as the arrow combines the fletching, the nock, and the blade.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: by your life (a sworn oath formula)
the inherited rights of the caliphate
the fletching, nock, and blade of an arrow (parts of a complete arrow)

context: A panegyric to al-Fadl ibn al-Rabi', the powerful Abbasid vizier, with a respectful gesture toward the Caliph al-Amin. Abu Nuwas praises al-Fadl as the true engine of governance while diplomatically acknowledging al-Amin's inherited supremacy. The closing metaphor of the arrow — fletching, nock, and blade uniting to make a complete weapon — is an elegant image for al-Fadl's comprehensive excellence.

titleAr: يا ربع شغلك إني عنك في شغل
titleEn: O Ruined Encampment, I Have My Own Concern

verses.sadr: يا ربع شغلك إني عنك في شغل
verses.ajuz: لا ناقتي فيك ، لو تدري ، ولا جملي

verses.sadr: يا فضل ، غاية خلق الله كلهم ،
كم قائل لك من داع وقائلة :
verses.ajuz: نفسي فداء أبي العباس من رجل

translationEn: O ruined encampment, your concern is your own — I am occupied with my own affair; neither my she-camel nor my he-camel is involved with you. Upon me is an eye and ear from a masculine one, connected to the desire of the lover of men and of ghazal. Both of them reach toward it with their aspiration, despite their difference in the place of work. O al-Fadl — the ultimate of all God's creation when we set generosity as the measure of the highest — how many a man and woman call out: 'My soul is a ransom for Abu al-Abbas, what a man!' They both ransom you with all their effort, and both pray for your term of life to be extended.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a ruined encampment, the trace of a former dwelling (traditional nasib opening)
one who loves men (a term for male-male desire, from Lot)
a masculine one, a male companion
aspiring with his ambition, reaching high
the ultimate example, the highest exemplar
the appointed term of life, one's lifespan

context: A panegyric to al-Fadl ibn al-Rabi' that opens with a bold rejection of the traditional qasida nasib (the elegiac prelude over a deserted campsite). Abu Nuwas dismisses the ruined camp as irrelevant to him — his concern is with poetry and desire, both of which he conflates. The explicit reference to homosexual desire alongside ghazal is characteristically frank, before pivoting to lavish praise of al-Fadl.

titleEn: O Lady of the Beautiful Face
translationEn: O lady of the beautiful face, and the mole upon the smooth cheek — be generous, even if only with what the miser's soul would grudge. With a small gift from you — for much grows from little. God delivered me, and through al-Fadl's judgment, from the fetters' grip. And He redeemed me from the hardship of the age, when I had despaired of any relief.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the mole, the beauty spot on the face
relieved, pardoned, delivered from a difficult situation
a place of rest, respite, relief

context: A short ghazal that pivots mid-poem from romantic address to gratitude toward al-Fadl ibn al-Rabi', who apparently secured Abu Nuwas's release from imprisonment. The abrupt shift from the beloved's mole to political deliverance is characteristic of the qasida-madih tradition embedded in shorter lyric forms. The poem testifies to Abu Nuwas's real-world dependence on patron relationships.

titleAr: أأسلمتني يا جعفر بن أبي الفضل
titleEn: Have You Abandoned Me, O Ja'far Son of Abu al-Fadl

verses.sadr: أأسلمتني يا جعفر بن أبي الفضل ،
verses.ajuz: فمن لي ، إذا أسلمتني يا أبا الفضل ؟

verses.sadr: وأي فتى في الناس أرجو مقامه ،
verses.ajuz: إذا أنت لم تفعل ، وأنت أخو الفضل

verses.sadr: فقل لأبي العباس إن كنت مذنبا ،
ولا تجحدوا بي ود عشرين حجة ،
verses.ajuz: ولا تفسدوا ما كان منكم من الفضل

translationEn: Have you abandoned me, O Ja'far son of Abu al-Fadl? Who will be there for me if you abandon me, O Abu al-Fadl? And which young man in all the world can I hope to stand in his place, if you do not act — you who are the brother of virtue? Tell Abu al-Abbas: if I am guilty, then you are the most deserving of people to take the path of magnanimity. Do not deny me twenty years of affection, and do not ruin what goodness there was from you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: have you abandoned me, left me to my fate
his standing, his position, his station
brother of virtue/excellence (also a play on 'al-Fadl' as a name)
taking the path of magnanimity, showing grace

context: A supplication poem addressed to Ja'far ibn al-Fadl al-Rabi', the son of the powerful vizier. Abu Nuwas pleads not to be abandoned after twenty years of relationship with the family. The poem repeatedly plays on the name 'al-Fadl' (meaning 'virtue' or 'excellence') to rhetorically bind the family to their own generous reputation. This is Abu Nuwas in his most vulnerable, petitioning mode.

titleEn: Pour the Wine Back to Me in the Cup
translationEn: Pour the wine back to me in the cup, and give it to me despite the reproach of my blamers. And drag a wineskin as if it were a man with thick forearms of Ham's lineage. Pour for us, pour an aged wine through which my thick-woven Islam becomes thin. It is as if, when the water strikes it, a shooting star blazing in clouds glows before me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: despite my blamers, in defiance of those who reproach me
a wineskin, a leather flask for wine
a shooting star in clouds, a bolt of light through overcast sky

context: A vivid khamriyyat in the munsarih meter. The poem moves rapidly from the command to pour, through a comically vivid image of dragging a fat wineskin, to the provocative line about wine thinning the poet's Islamic piety — one of Abu Nuwas's most startling formulations of the tension between wine and religion. The poem closes with the classic Abu Nuwas image of wine mixed with water producing a flash of light like a shooting star.

titleAr: يا رب ليل بت في نعمة
titleEn: O Lord, Many a Night I Spent in Bliss

verses.sadr: يا رب ليل بت في نعمة ،

translationEn: O Lord, many a night I spent in bliss beside a white-skinned, smiling young man — by a cupbearer handsome of face, just in the pouring, no oppressor. He kept filling me with a potent wine that flowed from the ewer into the cup.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a potent wine; literally antidote, used for strong drink

context: A short khamriyyat epigram typical of Abu Nuwas's tavern scenes: a beautiful pale-skinned cupbearer serves potent wine through the night. The poem's brevity concentrates its sensory pleasure — the glowing face, the precise pour, the strong draught.

titleEn: Wanderers Who Lost Their Way

verses.sadr: فلاحت لهم منا على النأي قهوة ،

translationEn: And many a band of travellers who had lost their way after darkness's horizon crowded upon them — they cocked their ears toward a sound, for we were a company with a youth humming from his drunkenness. Then from afar our wine shone out to them, its glow like the light of a blazing fire. Whenever they sipped it pure, they stayed in place; but when it was diluted with water, they spurred their mounts and departed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine (older meaning; later came to mean coffee)
they sipped it, drank it in small sips

context: An unusual khamriyyat in which the wine-party's glow serves as a beacon for lost night-travellers. The witty conceit at the end — that travellers stay when the wine is pure but flee when it is diluted — captures Abu Nuwas's conviction that only unmixed wine is worth drinking.

titleEn: The Wretch Wanders Over the Ruined Dwellings
translationEn: The wretch goes wandering over ruined dwellings, while wine is in my palm and I wander in delight — with a company of wine-hummers who set out at twilight on a dark night, its darkness tangled and pitch-black. Composed and dignified, their talk among themselves subdued, humming softly with a murmur whose inner meaning is clear. I sat with them in good fellowship, training myself in their refinement — for among the Persians the custom of their drinking is measured and fixed. For the Persian nobleman, souls are the noblest, and their boasting in company is absent. They called for the morning cup, and I said: what a noble scene! It was sweet, and sweet was its dear companion. In a garden where bliss played in its streams, leaving traces among the houses. To the right, arranged rivulets; to the left, orchards and vineyards. And when I carouse with an Arab group, the tribe of Tamim rushes to speak of glory; I turn to Qays and string their bow, then I capture Tamim and their whole company is routed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who hum while drinking, wine-singers
twilight, the dimness between light and dark
the morning drink, early morning wine
the Arab tribe of Tamim, famous for tribal boasting

context: A sophisticated khamriyyat that opens with a mock contrast between the love-poet wandering over ruins and Abu Nuwas wandering in wine-induced bliss. The poem then praises the refined Persian drinking culture over boastful Arab tribal behaviour, a recurring theme in Abu Nuwas's work that reflects his mixed Arabо-Persian background.

titleEn: My Eyes Refuse to Sleep Without You
translationEn: My eyes refused after you to sleep — how can one who harbours sickness sleep? I wept at parting for what I suffered, and returned to longing and passionate love. I went back to Iraq despite myself, and left behind al-Jazira and Syria. Upon the shore of Syria and its people: the greeting of a Muslim who has met his death. She is masculine and feminine both, a white oryx — when she comes forth, she resembles a young man. She disdains water and clarified honey, and drinks wine out of her youthfulness. She tells her sword: O sword, rejoice! You will be sated with blood and will cleave a skull. And one who spoke to her as a sincere advisor said: Why did you slay this bewildered lover? Her answer, with a charming touch: Shall I combine his face and unlawfulness? Truly profitable is the trade of every lovesick one whom his beloved gifts with greetings.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: white oryx; metaphor for a beautiful woman
wine, aged wine (from دام, to persist)
the bewildered lover, one maddened by love

context: A ghazal of departure and longing composed when Abu Nuwas was compelled to return from Syria to Iraq. The beloved is described in androgynous terms — both masculine and feminine, like a warrior and an oryx — a favourite trope in Abu Nuwas's erotic poetry. The final witty exchange between the beloved and her reproacher gives the poem a characteristic lightness.

titleAr: كان حلما ما كنت آمل فيكم
titleEn: What I Hoped From You Was a Dream

verses.sadr: كان حلما ما كنت آمل فيكم ،
بلغوا ما أقول من لا أسمي ،

translationEn: What I hoped from you was a dream, and dreams rarely come true. Convey what I say to the one I will not name — many a word heals sickness. News has reached me of your turning away from me, and of barbs like arrows. You have replaced us with another friend, while any other than you is forbidden upon my heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: forbidden; here: someone else is spiritually impermissible to his heart

context: A brief ghazal of separation and reproach. The poet learns that his beloved has abandoned him for another, yet insists that no one else can take that person's place in his heart. The paradox that both parting and replacement are impossible gives the poem its emotional intensity.

titleAr: يا رب إن عظمت ذنوبي كثرة
titleEn: O Lord, Though My Sins Are Great in Number

verses.sadr: يا رب إن عظمت ذنوبي ، كثرة ،
إن كان لا يرجوك إلا محسن ،

verses.ajuz: فإذا رددت يدي ، فمن ذا يرحم

verses.sadr: ما لي إليك وسيلة إلا الرجا ،

translationEn: O Lord, though my sins are great in their multitude, I know well that Your pardon is greater. If only the righteous may hope in You, then in whom does the criminal take refuge and shelter? I call upon You, O Lord, as You commanded, in humble supplication — if You turn my hand away, who then will show mercy? I have no means to You save hope, and the beauty of Your pardon, and the fact that I am a Muslim.
context: One of Abu Nuwas's most celebrated zuhdiyyat (pious poems), famous for its theological directness. The poet does not claim righteousness; instead he argues that precisely because he is a sinner, he is most in need of divine mercy. This poem is often cited as evidence of Abu Nuwas's sincere religious sensibility beneath his libertine exterior.

titleEn: Leave Your Side to the Archer
translationEn: Leave your side open to the archer and pass by him in peace. Die of the sickness of silence — it is better for you than the sickness of speech. Many times a jest has unlocked the bolts of death. Many a word has driven the fates upon those asleep and those awake. The only one who is safe is he who has bridled his mouth with a bit. So clothe yourself with people whether in their health or their sickness. Hold to moderation, for moderation preserves one the most against death. You have grown old, yet you do not abandon the ways of a boy. And the fates are ever-eating, ever-drinking of humankind!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fates, appointed times of death (pl. of أجل)

context: A wisdom poem in the mujazza' al-ramal metre, structured as direct moral counsel. Its core argument — that silence is safer than speech, and that an ill-timed jest can bring death — reflects the classical Arabic virtue of knowing when to hold one's tongue. The final image of the fates as creatures that eat and drink humankind gives the poem its memorable dark power.

titleEn: My Heart Is Patient and My Tongue Is Silent

verses.sadr: فطرفي الذي قاد الفؤاد إلى الهوى ،
verses.ajuz: ألا إن طرفي ، ما علمت ، مشوم

verses.sadr: دعاه الهوى ، فانقاد طوعا إلى الهوى ،

verses.ajuz: ولا كان في دار الحبيب رحيم

translationEn: My heart is patient and my tongue is silent, yet my tears are informers of my heart's secrets. When I said weeping has consumed it, tears poured down, streaming copiously. My eye — it was my eye that led my heart to passion; truly my eye, as I have known it, is ill-omened. Passion called it, and it willingly submitted to passion — and the caller of passion is a soft-voiced, nasal gazelle. All I desire of this wide world is a beautiful girl, and her desire in fate is excess. She is the sun in her radiance, a diver's pearl, the musk preserved in a perfumer's jar, and a white oryx. I swore by God that I love her, and not every oath sworn for love's sake is a sin. She showed me no mercy when I complained of my longing, and no mercy dwelt in the beloved's house. I asked Abu 'Isa — the most perfect of sane men — for ignorance and knowledge are not equal. I said: I see myself — not you — as if I were Sulaim! He replied: The lovesick one is indeed Sulaim.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an informer, a betrayer of secrets
with a nasal, melodious voice (describing a gazelle-like beauty)
literally 'unharmed'; ironically used for a lovesick person (folk etymology)
one maddened or bewildered by love

context: A ghazal of contradiction: the heart is patient, the tongue silent, yet the tears speak. The poem builds to a witty climax in which Abu Nuwas consults a wise man (Abu 'Isa) and is told that the lovesick man is 'Salim' — playing on the Arabic word that means both 'safe/unharmed' and is a common name, used ironically for someone bitten by a serpent (love).

titleEn: I Die and You Don't Know, Though You Killed Me

verses.ajuz: فلا أنا أبديها ، ولا أنت تعلم

verses.sadr: ولو لم يبح دمعي بمكنون حبكم ،

translationEn: I die, and you do not know it, though you are the one who killed me — I do not reveal it, and you do not know. My tongue and my heart conceal my love for you, yet my tears speak of the passion. And even if my tears had not revealed the secret of my love for you, my wasted body would speak, translating it through its thinness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: translates, interprets, speaks on behalf of
I reveal it, I make it apparent

context: A compact ghazal on the paradox of concealed love: the heart and tongue keep the secret, but tears and the wasting body are involuntary confessors. The three-verse structure tightens the argument with each line — silence, then tears, then the body itself — making it one of Abu Nuwas's most elegantly compressed love poems.

titleEn: One Whose Glance Complies Yet Whose Tongue Is Chaste

verses.sadr: قائم في الوهم ، حتى إذا ما
فهي سن الدهر إن هي فرت ،

translationEn: One whose glance complies yet whose tongue is chaste — encouraging by downcast eyes, rebellious in his will — teasing me with hope mixed with despair, distant in deed and word yet near. When earnestness speaks to you of him, earnestness gives the lie to desire's whisper. Yet I say what comes to me of suppositions, belying the evidence of sight. I force myself to compose something: one in word but manifold in meaning — existing in the imagination, yet when I aim at it I aim at a riddle of place. As if I follow some beauty before me that is not clearly seen. So I consoled myself with pure wine, raised in the lap of time's mother. It is time's own age: if they fled, they grew up together and suckled from one breast. And the two perpetual renewers forgot it, until it became half-portions of jars.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one whose glance is compliant, yielding in looks
rebellious in the rein, wilfully disobedient
a riddle, an enigma, something obscure
day and night, the two alternating renewers of time

context: A dense ghazal-khamriyyat that moves from an introspective portrait of the beloved's contradictory nature — compliant eyes, resistant will — to a philosophical meditation on the elusiveness of beauty, and then to wine as the only reliable consolation. The final image of wine aged until the jars are half-empty suggests its ancient, primordial quality.

titleAr: أعلم أن لا خير لي عندكم
titleEn: I Know There Is No Good for Me With You

verses.sadr: أعلم أن لا خير لي عندكم ،

translationEn: I know there is no good for me with you — for my messenger came back in anger. Had there been good news, he would have brought it to me first and come to me laughing with joy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he would have come to me first with it

context: A miniature ghazal epigram of two verses — a form Abu Nuwas mastered. The poet reads the messenger's mood as the true message: an angry return signals rejection. The brevity is itself the technique, enacting the swift, deflating recognition of unwelcome news.

titleEn: Lovelier Than the Description of Ruined Traces

verses.sadr: من كف ظبي أغن ، ذي غنج ،

translationEn: Lovelier than the description of eroded ruins, than a dove weeping on a branch, than deserted dwellings whose landmarks have faded — is a sprig of sweet basil tucked behind the ear, in a garden lush with blooming plants hemmed with every bright beauty. Its embroidered patterns are like the embroidery of robes spread out in Yemen. And a wine with no impurity mixing into it, coming to you from its origin and its source, from a wine-seller's house — she brings it to you like a bride from her homeland. Its potency rises to the head, and its smoothness in the mouth is like oil. From the hand of a soft-voiced, coquettish gazelle in whom beauty's finest rarities are made manifest — he strides forth with a golden cup, like carnelian in the goblet, draped in a cloud of foam. That scene surpasses in bliss what Dea'bel describes, and the descriptions of ruins and desolate traces.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruined, blackened traces of old encampments
impurity, spec in the eye or wine
carnelian, red stone; also a thin red cloud
sash, shoulder-belt; here the froth on wine
the poet Dea'bel al-Khuzai, famous for elegies on ruins

context: A manifesto-poem of Abu Nuwas's aesthetic programme: the pleasures of wine, nature, and a beautiful cupbearer are explicitly declared lovelier than the traditional elegies over ruins. The direct mention of Dea'bel (a contemporary) makes this a pointed literary statement, not merely a celebration of drinking.

titleEn: As for the Dwellings, They Seldom Tarried There
translationEn: As for the dwellings, they seldom tarried in them, racing their mounts with their riders. They laid the whips of longing upon their necks until they carried them forth beyond their homelands.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: riders, travellers on mount (pl. of راكب)
they brought them forth, they raised them up

context: A two-verse fragment that plays with the nasib (elegiac opening) convention. Rather than lamenting the departed, Abu Nuwas characterises longing itself as the whip that drove the travellers away — transforming the traditional elegiac dwelling into a point of departure rather than loss.

titleEn: We Withdrew from People When They Grew Aware

verses.ajuz: إن كان لي في ديارهم سكن

translationEn: We withdrew from people when they became aware, yet between us, when we meet, all is beauty. We pushed away the matter when it was first kindled, and it grew until they have now grown accustomed to it. No eye sees impurity in what it witnesses of it, nor does any ear reject it. Woe to Thaqif! What harm comes to them if I have a dwelling in their quarters? What is most between us is conversation, and if we add to it, add away — and that has no price.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we withdrew, we kept apart, we avoided
they grew aware, they figured it out
they grew accustomed, they became hardened to it
to have a speck (of dust) fall in the eye; to be troubled
the tribe of Thaqif; refers to a specific locale

context: A ghazal of clandestine love made public through habituation. The poem traces a social arc: the lovers first withdraw when others notice, but over time the relationship becomes so familiar that no one is troubled by it. The wry reference to the tribe of Thaqif grounds an otherwise abstract love poem in a specific social setting.

titleEn: My Beloved Is Unjust to Me and Withholding
translationEn: My beloved is unjust to me and withholding — I seek help from my Lord against his injustice. He is precious to me, yet by God's grace I am insignificant to him. If only I knew: is your heart, that refuses to soften, made of rock? When I complain of love as the wretched and submissive complain, he says: Was all this something you saw in sleep? Then you saw something good, and good shall it be!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I seek help, I implore assistance
the one who submits humbly, the abject
he is dear, he is precious to me
I am insignificant, I am lesser (in his eyes)

context: A ghazal of unrequited love in which the beloved's cruelty is real but his beauty makes it bearable. The dismissive final response — 'was that a dream? Then good!' — captures the beloved's indifference with cutting wit. The poem's invocation of God at the start gives it an unusual devotional flavour for a love poem.

titleEn: One Whose Temples Are Curled Like a Scorpion's Tail

verses.sadr: يا ذا الذي نقض العهود ، وملني ،
verses.ajuz: ما كنت أعلم أن ذا سيكون !

translationEn: One whose temples are curled like a scorpion's tail — his glances are sorcery, and in him is elegance and audacity. Rose-cheeked: his touch is as precious as aloeswood, while his heart is hard as stone. Our eyes harvest the beauties of his face, so every young man's heart is captivated by him. When the sun sets, I am lit by his face, and he is seen in place of the full moon when it appears. I stole kisses from him sweeter than all wishes — my heart is pledged for them until death. O you who broke your vows and grew weary of me, I never knew that this would come to pass!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one whose temple-locks curl like a scorpion's tail
aloeswood, a rare aromatic wood of great value
I stole secretly from him, I snatched by stealth

context: A ghazal portrait of a beautiful youth whose temple-locks curl like a scorpion's tail — a standard image of Arab beauty aesthetics. The poem moves from physical description to emotional captivation, and ends with a lament at betrayal. The contrast between the hard heart and the soft, rose-like complexion is characteristic of Abu Nuwas's beloved-portraits.

titleEn: Alert of Glance Beneath Drowsy Eyelids

verses.ajuz: دعصا من الرمل في غصن من البان

translationEn: Alert of glance beneath drowsy eyelids — I kissed his mouth and he greeted me with sweet basil. His beautiful appearance enslaves all wishes; pure of conscience, yet his glance is an adornment. His glance has not reached other people's eyes — where all secret and open are alike. O you whose maker took great care and fashioned you: a mound of sand in a branch of the ban tree.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sweet basil, fragrant herb; also any sweet breath
a mound or heap of sand
the ban tree (Moringa peregrina), symbol of supple beauty

context: A ghazal of a beautiful youth whose sleepy eyelids conceal an alert gaze — a paradox central to Arab beauty ideals. The final image compares his body to a sand-dune supported by a ban-tree branch: softness and suppleness combined. The reference to his maker 'taking great care' (تأنق) is a compliment to divine craftsmanship.

titleEn: Your Love, O Ahmad, Has Wasted Me
translationEn: Your love, O Ahmad, has wasted me — O moon in the body of a human. O rose hastened by a plucking hand that passed by it through the gate of Uthman.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: has wasted me, has made me ill with longing
a plucking hand, one who picks flowers
the Gate of Uthman; a topographical reference in a city
a rose; metaphor for a delicate beautiful person

context: A miniature ghazal addressed to Ahmad — one of many named beloveds in Abu Nuwas's poetry. The rose hastened by a picker and passing through a city gate gives the poem a fleeting, urban quality: the beloved appears and vanishes as quickly as a flower is plucked.

titleAr: لم أزل أخلع في الحب الرسن
titleEn: I Have Never Ceased Casting Off Love's Halter

verses.sadr: لم أزل أخلع في الحب الرسن ،

translationEn: I have never ceased casting off love's halter, while my heart is held in pledge by a gazelle. My eyelids pour their tears, and within my breast grief has filled its interior. Ever since I saw a rising crescent moon, swaying with a figure like a branch. His mim pierced my heart in love, and by his ha my heart was captivated. And by the mim after it he disquieted me, and by his dal he drew my soul out of my body.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: halter, rope (for an animal); metaphor for restraint
the inward parts, bowels; seat of emotion
a rising crescent moon (metaphor for the beloved's face)
drew out, extracted; salled روحي = drew my soul

context: A ghazal that ends with an alphabetic wordplay: the letters م (mim), ح (ha), م (mim), د (dal) spell the name محمد (Muhammad), the poet's beloved. This device of spelling out a name through its letters' effects on the body is a playful conceit Abu Nuwas employed for several named loves.

titleEn: People Have Prepared for the Festival
translationEn: People have prepared for the festival all sorts of pleasures, while I have prepared with my tears wine and sweet basil. O you to whom the world listens when you are angry — abandon the shunning you have been visiting upon me as you once were. How unseemly for the beloved to sometimes abandon the lover, when the beloved is no table of provision for the lover.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a table of food, a spread; metaphor for provision and care

context: A festival ghazal: while everyone else prepares pleasures for the Eid, the poet prepares only wine and sweet basil — and appeals to his beloved to end the estrangement. The paradox in the final couplet — that the beloved who abandons the lover is no 'table of provision' — gives the poem a pointed, witty close.

titleEn: He Made Estrangement Apparent After Union
translationEn: He made estrangement apparent after union, and turned pretexts into allies. He counts my kindnesses as offences, just as I count his offences as forgiveness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my kindness, my goodness toward him

context: A sharp two-verse epigram on the perversity of the beloved's logic: the lover's kindnesses are counted as sins, while the beloved's sins are pardoned by the lover. The neat chiasmic reversal in the second verse condenses a complex emotional asymmetry into a single elegant sentence.

titleEn: O Night I Spent Being Given to Drink

verses.sadr: كان لها الدهر من أب خلفا ،

translationEn: O night I spent being given wine to drink — its sweetness made me passionate in recalling it! We take it at times and it takes us, a grudge we demand and then reopen. We overpower it first, then it overpowers us — we are its horsemen and its fallen. The hand blazes from its blazing, and the eye is strained in trying to fathom it. As though a fire had been kindled within it, we dread it at times and at times approach it. Time had nursed it like a father's successor, protecting and raising it in his lap. In a garden where spring's first blossoms were its companions, its lavender neighboured its buttercup. We have minstrel-girls who choose for us songs, while our ears remain their mounts. A cup-girl in a short tunic kept moving the cup along — had beauty been made mortal it would not have surpassed her. My eye and her eye share a language whose words contradict their meaning. When my glance demands of her a promise, I know its reply by its unspoken import. This is a language to which all other languages bow — it was made cryptic by a lover and obscured by his blindness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a blood-feud grudge, something owed in revenge
those she has felled, her fallen wrestlers
wearing a short tunic, clad in a qurtaq garment

context: One of Abu Nuwas's most elaborate khamriyyat, structured as a memory of a single magnificent wine-night. The central extended metaphor is a wrestling match with wine — first the drinkers win, then the wine wins. The poem culminates in a silent language of glances between the poet and the cupbearer-girl, a language purer than words.

titleEn: O You Who Reproach Me Over Wine
translationEn: O you who reproach me over wine — when did you become a fool? I used to think you were capable of something other than this as counsel. If we obeyed this reproach, we would be obeying God regarding it. So drink the morning cup of wine, O my companion, and pour it for me — for when people blame me over it, I crave it all the more.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the reproacher, the one who blames
a fool, a simpleton, one who lacks judgment
the morning drink (اصطبح = drink the morning cup)

context: A short khamriyyat in the mujazza' al-ramal metre that inverts the reproach: Abu Nuwas claims that obeying the reproacher would mean obeying God, which is absurd — so he dismisses the reprover as a fool. The final twist, that people's blame actually increases his craving for wine, is a characteristic paradox.

titleEn: I Was Alone With Wine, Whispering to It
translationEn: I was alone with wine, whispering to it — taking from it and giving back. I kept it company as a drinking companion when I found no friend whom I was pleased to have share it with me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was alone, I had privacy
I whispered to it, spoke to it intimately
I exchanged with it, I gave and received
I kept it company, drank with it as a companion
whom I was pleased with, who satisfied me

context: A very short khamriyyat in which wine becomes the sole companion: the poet anthropomorphises the cup, whispering to it and exchanging with it as with an intimate. The poem's two verses distil Abu Nuwas's fundamental relationship with wine — preferring solitary drinking with the cup to sharing it with an unworthy companion.

titleEn: O Night I Spent in Its Darknesses

verses.sadr: ما تشتهي العين أن ترى حسنا ،
سقيا لذا الوصف حيث كان ، ولا

translationEn: O night I spent in its darknesses, given to drink the pure clarity of wine — our cup circled in good fortune swiftly, musk ground fine throughout its quarters. Whatever beauty the eye wished to see, it saw in the hand of its cupbearer. A serving-girl like a youth, fit for both roles, swaying like a branch. In a short tunic adorned by her Persian embroidery, her temples curled like scorpion-tails by her combs. God completed her and then said to her, when she had reached the full of her beauty: Enough! Had Beauty itself been asked to describe her beauties, it would have been too weak to portray them. Drink a cup from her hand — yet she has a cup of sickness that she pours into the soul. Until when intoxication bridled her pride and softened her sharp edges thereafter — she made herself available to me by artful stealth, and I gently stretched my hand to her mouth. She turned away then and trembled, and I took hold of her to placate her. She said: Was it for this you visited us? I said to her: O most beautiful of all people in your pride — had it not been for my affliction I would not have risked the deaths that dwell in her vicinity, nor exposed myself to perils with a soul that only a part of passion used to console. Welcome and peace to one whom my soul follows and who was among its wishes. So I spent a night I enjoyed — kissing her at times and giving her wine — gathering sweetness from her sweet parts and giving the soul its wishes. Rain upon this description wherever it may be, and no rain upon a house whose dwellings have become desolate.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its darknesses, dense dark (pl. of داجية)
a short tunic, a Persian garment (qurtaq)
by artful stealth, by cunning deception
perils, fates, deadly dangers (pl. of حتف)
its dwelling-places, its abodes (pl. of مغنى)

context: A major khamriyyat built around a night of wine and encounter with a beautiful androgynous cupbearer. The poem is notable for its dramatic interior scene — wine, musk, the wavering resistance and eventual yielding of the serving-girl — and culminates in a celebration of lived pleasure against the elegiac convention of lamenting ruins.

titleEn: No Youth Has Perfected Pleasures
translationEn: No youth has perfected pleasures save one who drinks with beardless youths as his boon-companions. This one redeems him with his self, and that one, when he passes him the wine, greets him. And whenever he longs for a kiss, he kisses one of them on the mouth. Rain upon a time when I kept their company — how sweet it was! We drank it pure without casting lots, and our condition was: whoever falls asleep, we take him!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: beardless youths (pl. of أمرد), adolescent boys
his boon-companions at drinking (pl. of نديم)
he redeems him, offers himself as ransom for him
we cast lots, we drew lots

context: A vivid khamriyyat celebrating wine-drinking in the company of beardless youths (murd), a characteristic setting in Abu Nuwas's tavern poetry. The poem ends with a humorous rule of the drinking-session: whoever falls asleep loses. The nostalgia for this time — 'how sweet it was!' — marks a moment of retrospective pleasure.

titleEn: Leave Me From the Dwelling — Let Me Weep Over It

verses.ajuz: إذا خلت من حبيب لي مغانيها

verses.sadr: إن كان فيها الذي أهوى أقمت بها ،
أمكنت عاذلتي في الخمر من أذن ،
أقول لما أدار الكأس لي قثم :
يا ألبق الناس كفا حين يمزجها ،
قد قمت فيها على حد يوافقنا ،
إذا نظرت إليه تاه عن نظري ،

verses.ajuz: بظلمة الليل أو قد كاد يضويها

translationEn: Leave me to weep over the dwelling and elegise it when its abodes are empty of my beloved. Let the dust-laden winds erase whatever traces of it remain, and let the rains weep over it. If the one I love is there, I shall stay; if he has passed by it, I shall soon despise it. A dwelling achieves its rank by abandonment when it is emptied of love's attachment to its people. I gave my reprover of wine an ear whose echo answers whoever calls to it. When Qutham circled the cup for me, I said: Now the bow is being strung by its maker! O most fitting of people when your hand mixes it and when you drink it pure and pour it out. You have maintained exactly the right measure in it that suits us — so pass it among us, then! If wine is a stealer of minds, then your eyes move in the same channels. In your glances are the qualities of sorcery, speaking in a single word of many meanings. So drink, that you may hope to attain its intoxication — for the point, if intoxication favours us, lies therein. And one of slender waist, ample in his haunches, swaying in a robe whose hems are fine. When I look at him he vanishes from my sight, and if he increases in coquetry he increases my bewilderment. I gave him the cup to drink while the light of dawn stretched to meet the darkness of night, nearly overwhelming it — a cup as though the creeping of ants was its gentleness, healing the one it stings when the enchanter breathes upon him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: dust-laden winds that erase traces (pl. of رامسة)
my female reprover, the one who blames me
Qutham, a name; here the cupbearer's name
one whose waist is slender, wasp-waisted
he sways, moves with a swaying grace
the creeping of ants; metaphor for a wine's gentle sensation
its enchanter, the one who blows a charm upon it

context: A long khamriyyat that opens by parodying the elegiac nasib (weeping over ruins), then pivots to a brilliant scene of drinking with a named cupbearer, Qutham, whose eyes are compared to wine in their capacity to steal reason. The poem ends at dawn with a cup of wine so subtle it is compared to the creeping of ants.

titleEn: Fadl Has Grown Openly Arrogant
translationEn: Fadl has grown openly arrogant — and that is since I began to satirize him. By God, what an eloquent mouth — my rhymes for everyone beneath me! How different is Fadl since I satirized him from what he was before I satirized him. Praise be to God, though I cared nothing for those who advised me concerning him. I am satisfied that a fallen wretch insults me — my sandal-strap is better than his patrons. And this is no stranger than that: a slave-girl of al-Nitaf courts him, and the plague of al-Nitaf is from an anger — she enraged him one day, so he came to her — until when I stood at his door, she named me to the people as one of his whores.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I satirize him, I compose lampoons against him
my sandal-strap; used as the most trivial thing
al-Nitaf; a quarter or tavern district (toponym)
his prostitutes, his low women (pl. of زانية)

context: A satirical poem (hija) against a rival named Fadl. Abu Nuwas boasts that his satirical verses have transformed Fadl from obscurity to notoriety — making him arrogant by giving him a name. The poem descends into a scurrilous attack on Fadl's association with a slave-girl and a disreputable quarter, ending with a sharp insult.

titleAr: لا تفرغ النفس من شغل بدنياها
titleEn: The Soul Never Empties of Its Worldly Preoccupations

verses.sadr: لا تفرغ النفس من شغل بدنياها ،
يا بؤس جلد على عظم مخرقة ،
يرى عليك به فضلا يبين به ،
verses.ajuz: إن نال في العاجل السلطان والجاها

verses.sadr: أنت اللئيم الذي لم تعد همته ،
يا راكب الذنب ، قد شابت مفارقه ،

translationEn: The soul never empties of its worldly preoccupations — I have seen it never reaching what it desires. We sigh for a world that is departing, though we can be content with its least portion. I warn you against pride — let not its brand-mark cling to you, for it is a garment that God will strip away. Woe to a skin stretched over hollow bones, full of holes — when you address it, it grows vain. It sees in it a superiority over you made plain if it gains in this life power and prestige. Praising itself, satisfied with its own conduct — you have lied, O servant and master of this world. I despise my own soul when it waxes proud — so how can I be safe from God's loathing of it? You are the base one whose ambitions have never gone beyond preferring this world: when it calls to him, he answers. O rider of sin, whose temples have gone grey — do you not fear from the days their consequence?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its pride, its haughtiness, its arrogance
the base, the ignoble, the mean-spirited person

context: A sustained zuhd (ascetic) poem in which Abu Nuwas attacks worldly pride (kibr) with medical and eschatological imagery. The metaphor of pride as a garment that God will tear off, and the grotesque image of skin stretched over hollow bones, are characteristic of the ascetic tradition. The poem closes with a direct address to the grey-templed sinner, making it urgent and personal.

titleEn: My Youth Has Passed and I Have Abandoned Pleasures

verses.ajuz: و ، ولا عذر في المقام لساه

translationEn: My youth has passed and I have abandoned pleasures, now that grey hair has struck my parting with calamities. My reason forbade me, and I inclined toward justice, and I feared the words of a forbidder. O heedless one who persists in frivolity — there is no excuse for persisting when one is forgetful. We cannot achieve salvation by our deeds on the day when the sky is made visible above the foreheads. Yet even so, amid my wrongdoing and my negligence, I hope in the beauty of God's pardon.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my youth, my prime; also youthful rashness
the heedless one, the one who is negligent
negligence, squandering, falling short of one's duty

context: A tawbah (repentance) poem marking the end of Abu Nuwas's wild youth. The arrival of grey hair is the trigger: reason reasserts itself and the poet turns toward rectitude. The final verse is characteristic of his religious poetry — he does not claim righteousness but places his hope entirely in divine grace, echoing the famous four-verse prayer poem (abu-nuwas-101).

titleAr: كم ليلة قد بت ألهو بها
titleEn: How Many a Night I Spent in Pleasure

verses.sadr: كم ليلة قد بت ألهو بها ،

translationEn: How many a night I spent in pleasure — had that amusement lasted, it would have made me forever lost in play. God had forbidden it, yet I made it lawful for myself. So how then shall I seek God's pardon?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I play, I amuse myself, I indulge in pleasure
one lost in play, the heedless pleasure-seeker
he forbade it, declared it unlawful (God as subject)
I made it lawful, I permitted it for myself

context: A short but piercing poem of repentance (tawba) in which Abu Nuwas confronts his own transgression with disarming candor. Rather than elaborate penitential rhetoric, he ends on a single, devastating question: how can he dare ask God's forgiveness when he knowingly violated the divine prohibition? The poem belongs to the zuhd and repentance strand that runs alongside Abu Nuwas's khamriyyat, showing the poet's unease beneath his libertine persona.

titleEn: I Set Out at Dawn While Darkness Still Lingers
translationEn: I set out at dawn while darkness still lingered, like the two bright marks on the hem of a cloak that signal daybreak — with a sparrowhawk that astonishes all who see it. Among all falcons, there is no equal to this sparrowhawk. From the dark blaze that has grown upon its cheeks, steel-blue, belied by nothing in its eyes. Were the hunter to see what it sees, he would sacrifice himself for it — father and mother both — and indeed he has. After the whites of its eyes have vanished, the reed-warbler's wings cannot shelter it, nor can two wings enfold it from the hawk once it takes flight in pursuit. Not until the magic is torn from within its breast — were it to recite endless glorifications of God, nothing would save it. That is the gift God has bestowed upon us. Blessed be God who guided it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I set out at early dawn, I depart at daybreak
sparrowhawk (a small, agile falcon used in hunting)
its equal, its match, its like
dark blaze or black mark on the face of a bird or animal
the whites of its two eyes
glorification of God, reciting subhan Allah
that which God has granted us as a gift or boon

context: A hunting poem (tardiyyah) in which Abu Nuwas describes an early morning falconry outing and glorifies the exceptional sparrowhawk he carries. The poem is notable for its precise ornithological vocabulary — the description of the hawk's blue-grey plumage, dark facial markings, and predatory focus show Abu Nuwas as an acute observer of nature. The closing couplet turns the poem toward pious gratitude, blessing God for the gift of such a bird, a characteristic Abu Nuwas pivot between worldly delight and religious acknowledgment.

titleAr: من يك من حبيك خلوا ، فما
titleEn: Though Others Be Free of Your Love

verses.sadr: من يك من حبيك خلوا ، فما

translationEn: Though others may be free of loving you, I for my part have not become free of your love. He calls out, honey-syrup in hand: who will buy sweetness from the sweet one? I said: sell me of it what I desire — and he walked on, hurrying, without looking back.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: free from, empty of, unburdened by (love)
honey-syrup candy, a confection; also a vendor of sweets
the sweet one; also sweetness, playing on both meanings
I desire, I long for, I crave
he turns back, he looks back, he lingers

context: A three-verse mujun and ghazal poem built around a pun: the sweet-vendor calling out 'who will buy sweetness from the sweet one?' (الحلو من الحلو) conflates the literal sweetness of his confection with the figurative sweetness of the beautiful youth carrying it. Abu Nuwas's bold reply and the boy's indifferent departure form a miniature drama of unrequited desire, typical of Abu Nuwas's urban ghazal in which the beloved is a merchant's boy encountered in the marketplace.

titleEn: O Fadl, You Have Entrusted Me with Wisdom

verses.sadr: إن ضاق عفوك ، وهو ذو سعة ،

translationEn: O Fadl, you have entrusted me with a piece of wisdom after which there is no error or forgetfulness. I have acquitted myself of what you held suspect — so let that acquittal through you bring me joy. Accept, O Abu al-Abbas, my excuse — for the word of a youth has a sweet taste. If your pardon, vast as it is, grows too narrow for my sake, then no pardon is truly wide. You are the one whose soul has grown accustomed to generosity, and nothing else occupies his heart. Your honor goes forth in its fullness, while wealth is like a wrung-out kernel, lean and spent.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you entrusted me with, you deposited in me
admonition, counsel, a piece of wisdom
you suspect, you doubt, you hold in suspicion
lean, worn thin, exhausted; a worn-out camel

context: An apology and eulogy addressed to al-Fadl ibn Yahya al-Barmaki (Abu al-Abbas), one of the powerful Barmakid ministers at the Abbasid court. Abu Nuwas seeks reconciliation after apparently having given offense, pleading the natural rashness of youth. The poem praises the addressee's proverbial generosity (السماح) — a defining virtue of the Barmakids — and the contrast between his enduring honor and the transience of wealth is a classical panegyric topos. The Barmakids were famous patrons who supported poets and scholars.

titleEn: What al-Ghalibi Has Met — What a Fate!

verses.sadr: من سلط الله يا حسين على

translationEn: What al-Ghalibi has endured — what a fate he has met! I placed my hands upon the very pulling-out of his soul. He upon whose heart God, O Husayn, has unleashed a poet — he is disgraced. Woe to Ghulbun, for he is wretched — how has he consented to humiliation and affliction? I filled him with dread and terror so long as I live, and even after he is gone. By God, by God, I will never speak to him — how can I address a young man who has been disgraced?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Ghalibi, the one of the Banu Ghalib (proper name/nisba)
the act of drawing out, pulling away; the death agony, final breath
his lifeblood, his soul, the core of his being
he was disgraced, humiliated, brought to shame
fear, apprehension, the state of being afraid

context: A fierce hija (satire) directed at a man called al-Ghalibi or Ghulbun, addressed to a third party named Husayn. Abu Nuwas boasts that he has destroyed his target's reputation through verse, drawing on the ancient Arab belief that a poet's satire had quasi-magical destructive power — to have a skilled poet 'unleashed' upon you was a genuine social catastrophe. The repeated oath 'By God, by God' and the refusal ever to speak to the disgraced man again underline the absolute social rupture that satire could enact in the Abbasid literary world.

titleEn: Something in Me Dies Every Day

verses.ajuz: وكم عسى من أن يدوم الحي

translationEn: Something in me dies every day, yet my body remains firm and alive. A person wears away in his unfolding and his folding up — and how long can the living one endure? The final remedy for an incurable illness is the cauterizing iron!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: it dies, it perishes, it ceases
he wears out, he deteriorates, he is consumed by time
his unfolding, his spreading out; his waking hours and active life
the folding up; sleep, rest, the dormant state of life
the incurable illness, the disease that baffles all treatment
cauterization, branding with a hot iron (the last resort in ancient medicine)

context: A three-verse zuhd poem meditating on the daily diminishment of life. The arresting opening — 'something in me dies every day' — captures the slow erosion of vitality even while the body outwardly persists. The final line invokes the famous Arabic proverb that cauterization is the last resort of medicine (آخر الدواء الكي), here applied to existential decline: when ordinary remedies fail, only the most drastic measure remains. The poem is characteristic of Abu Nuwas's zuhd verse — brief, aphoristic, and suffused with the Arabic wisdom tradition.
